title.zh: 卷五十八 虞傅蓋臧列傳
title.en: Volume 58: Biographies of Yu, Fu, Gai, Zang
title.raw: 卷五十八 虞傅蓋臧列傳 Volume 58: Biographies of Yu, Fu, Gai, Zang

sentences.zh: 虞詡字升卿，陳國武平人也。

translations.literal: Yu Xu, styled Shengqing, was a man of Wuping in the state of Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu, whose courtesy name was Shengqing, came from Wuping in the principality of Chen.

sentences.zh: [一]祖父經，為郡縣獄吏，案法平允，務存寬恕，每冬月上其狀，恆流涕隨之。

translations.literal: [Note 1] His grandfather Jing served as a prison clerk in the commandery and county; he applied the law fairly and equally and strove to preserve leniency and forgiveness. Every winter when he submitted his case reports up the chain, he always wept as he accompanied them.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Jing had been a clerk in the district jail; he judged cases evenhandedly and took care to be merciful. Each winter when he forwarded his written verdicts, he would follow them with tears.

sentences.zh: 嘗稱曰：「東海於公高為里門，而其子定國卒至丞相。

translations.literal: He once declared: "In the East Sea [region], the Elder Yu raised his village gate, and his son Dingguo in the end reached the rank of Chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: He had once said: "When Elder Yu of Donghai built his village gate high, his son Yu Dingguo eventually rose to chancellor."

sentences.zh: [二]吾決獄六十年矣，雖不及於公，其庶幾乎！

translations.literal: [Note 2] I have decided cases for sixty years; though I do not match Elder Yu, perhaps I am close!
translations.idiomatic: [Note 2] “I have heard cases for sixty years; I may not measure up to Elder Yu, but I hope I come close.”

sentences.zh: 子孫何必不為九卿邪？」

translations.literal: Why should his descendants not become one of the Nine Ministers?"
translations.idiomatic: Why should their descendants not rise to the Nine Ministers?"

sentences.zh: 故字詡曰升卿。

translations.literal: Therefore he gave Xu the style Shengqing.
translations.idiomatic: So he gave the boy the courtesy name Shengqing—"rising minister."

sentences.zh: 注[一]武平故城在今亳州鹿邑縣東北。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The old city of Wuping is northeast of Luyi County in modern Bozhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The site of old Wuping lies northeast of present-day Luyi County in Bozhou.

sentences.zh: 酈元水經注雲武平城西南七里有漢尚書令虞詡碑，題云「君諱詡，字定安，虞仲之後」。

translations.literal: Li Daoyuan's Commentary on the Water Classic says that seven li southwest of Wuping city is a stele to the Han Minister of the Masters of Writing Yu Xu, whose inscription reads: "The gentleman's taboo name is Xu, his style Ding'an, a descendant of Yu Zhong."
translations.idiomatic: Li Daoyuan’s *Shui jing zhu* records a Han stele to Grandee Secretary Yu Xu seven li southwest of Wuping, which gives his name as Xu, his other style as Ding’an, and calls him a descendant of Yu Zhong.

sentences.zh: 定安蓋詡之別字也。

translations.literal: Ding'an was probably Xu's alternate style.
translations.idiomatic: Ding’an was apparently another courtesy name for Yu Xu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書，於定國字曼倩，東海人。

translations.literal: Comment [2] The previous book [Hanshu]: Yu Dingguo, styled Manqian, was a man of the East Sea.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Hanshu* says Yu Dingguo, courtesy name Manqian, was from Donghai.

sentences.zh: 其父於公為縣獄吏、郡決曹，所決皆不恨，為之生立祠。

translations.literal: His father, Elder Yu, was county prison clerk and commandery chief of the bureau of decisions; those he judged bore no resentment, and for him they erected a shrine while he still lived.
translations.idiomatic: His father, Elder Yu, had served as county jailer and commandery chief clerk for criminal decisions; no one resented his verdicts, and the people built him a living shrine.

sentences.zh: 其門閭壞，父老方共修之，於公曰：「少高大閭門，令容駟馬高蓋車。

translations.literal: His gate tower was broken, and the elders were together repairing it; Elder Yu said: "Raise the village gate a little higher, so it can admit a four-horse carriage with a tall canopy.
translations.idiomatic: When the gate needed repair and the elders were rebuilding it, Elder Yu told them: "Make the lane gate higher—wide enough for a four-in-hand with a tall canopy.

sentences.zh: 我決獄多陰德，未嘗有所冤，子孫必有興者。

translations.literal: In deciding cases I have accumulated hidden virtue and never wronged anyone; my descendants will surely rise."
translations.idiomatic: My judgments have built up hidden merit—I have never convicted the innocent. Our line will prosper."

sentences.zh: 」至定國為丞相，孫永為御史大夫也。

translations.literal: " Down to when Dingguo became Chancellor and his grandson Yong became Imperial Clerk.
translations.idiomatic: It came true: Dingguo reached the chancellorship, and his grandson Yong became imperial clerk."

sentences.zh: 詡年十二，能通尚書。

translations.literal: At age twelve Xu could master the Documents.
translations.idiomatic: At twelve he had mastered the *Book of Documents*.

sentences.zh: 早孤，孝養祖母。

translations.literal: He was orphaned young and filially supported his grandmother.
translations.idiomatic: Orphaned early, he devoted himself to caring for his grandmother.

sentences.zh: 縣舉順孫，國相奇之，欲以為吏。

translations.literal: The county nominated him as an exemplary grandson; the state Chancellor thought him remarkable and wished to appoint him clerk.
translations.idiomatic: The county recommended him as a model grandson; the kingdom’s chancellor was impressed and wanted to give him a post.

sentences.zh: 詡辭曰：「祖母九十，非詡不養。

translations.literal: Xu declined, saying: "My grandmother is ninety; without Xu she cannot be cared for.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu declined. “My grandmother is ninety; I alone can support her.

sentences.zh: 」相乃止。

translations.literal: " The Chancellor then desisted.
translations.idiomatic: ” The chancellor dropped the idea.”

sentences.zh: 後祖母終，服闋，辟太尉李修府，拜郎中。

translations.literal: Later when his grandmother died and mourning was completed, he was summoned to Grand Commandant Li Xiu's office and appointed Gentleman of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: After she died and he had finished mourning, he was called to the office of Grand Commandant Li Xiu and appointed gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]漢官儀曰：「修字伯游，襄城人也。」

translations.literal: [Note 1] Comment [1] Han Official Regulations says: "Xiu's style is Boyou; he was a man of Xiangcheng."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Han guan yi* gives Li Xiu’s courtesy name as Boyou and says he was from Xiangcheng.

sentences.zh: 永初四年，羌胡反亂，殘破並、涼，大將軍鄧騭以軍役方費，事不相贍，欲□涼州，並力北邊，乃會公卿集議。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yongchu, the Qiang and Hu rebelled and ravaged Bing and Liang; Grand General Deng Zhi, because military service was costly and affairs could not be mutually sustained, wished to [discard] Liangzhou and combine strength on the northern frontier, and therefore convened the dukes and ministers for joint deliberation.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongchu 4 (110 CE) the Qiang and frontier peoples rose in revolt and laid waste to Bing and Liang. Grand General Deng Zhi, seeing the burden of the campaigns, proposed abandoning Liang Province to concentrate forces on the northern border, and called a meeting of the high officials.

sentences.zh: 騭曰：「譬若衣敗，壞一以相補，猶有所完。

translations.literal: Zhi said: "It is like a garment worn out: if you tear one part to patch another, something can still remain whole.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Zhi said, “It is like a worn-out coat: you sacrifice one piece to patch another, and the whole can still hold together.

sentences.zh: 若不如此，將兩無所保。

translations.literal: If it is not thus, then both will be unable to be preserved.
translations.idiomatic: If we do not, we may lose both.”

sentences.zh: 」議者鹹同。

translations.literal: " Those who deliberated all agreed.
translations.idiomatic: The debaters all concurred.

sentences.zh: 詡聞之，乃說李修曰：「竊聞公卿定策當□涼州，求之愚心，未見其便。

translations.literal: When Xu heard of it, he then remonstrated with Li Xiu, saying: "I have privately heard that the dukes and ministers have decided a policy to [discard] Liangzhou; in my foolish mind I do not see the advantage.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu heard this and pressed Li Xiu: “I understand the court means to give up Liang Province. I see no good in that.”

sentences.zh: 先帝開拓土字，劬勞後定，而今憚小費，舉而□之。

translations.literal: The former emperors opened the territory and toiled to settle it later; now we fear small expense and in one move [discard] it.
translations.idiomatic: Our late sovereigns won that land at great cost, and we would throw it away to save a little money.”

sentences.zh: 涼州既□，即以三輔為塞；

translations.literal: Once Liangzhou is [discarded], then the Three Adjuncts will become the frontier barrier;
translations.idiomatic: If Liang goes, the capital region becomes the border;

sentences.zh: 三輔為塞，則園陵單外。

translations.literal: if the Three Adjuncts are the barrier, then the imperial tombs stand exposed beyond.
translations.idiomatic: and with the tombs of our emperors left naked on the outer rim.”

sentences.zh: 此不可之甚者也。

translations.literal: This is something that must not be done in the extreme.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing could be worse than that.”

sentences.zh: 喭曰：『關西出將，關東出相。

translations.literal: The proverb says: 'West of the Passes produces generals; east of the Passes produces chancellors.
translations.idiomatic: The saying runs: ‘West of the passes breeds commanders; east of them, ministers.’

sentences.zh: 』[一]觀其習兵壯勇，實過余州。

translations.literal: ' [1] Observing their practice of arms, vigor and bravery, they truly surpass the other provinces.
translations.idiomatic: ’ [1] In drill and courage they outmatch the other regions.”

sentences.zh: 今羌胡所以不敢入據三輔，為心腹之害者，以涼州在後故也。

translations.literal: Now the reason the Qiang and Hu do not dare enter and occupy the Three Adjuncts as a harm to the heart and belly is because Liangzhou lies behind.
translations.idiomatic: The Qiang do not dare push into the capital region because Liang lies at their rear.”

sentences.zh: 其土人所以推鋒執銳，無反顧之心者，為臣屬於漢故也。

translations.literal: The reason the native people thus advance spears and grasp keen blades without thought of turning back is because they are subjects belonging to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Its people fight without looking back because they are loyal subjects of Han.”

sentences.zh: 若□其境域，徙其人庶，安土重遷，必生異志。

translations.literal: If [we discard] its territory and relocate its people, they will cling to their soil and shrink from moving and will surely harbor divergent intentions.
translations.idiomatic: Deprive them of their land and resettle the population, and people who hate to leave home will turn against us.”

sentences.zh: 如使豪雄相聚，席捲而東，[二]雖賁、育為卒，太公為將，猶恐不足當御。

translations.literal: If heroes gather together and roll east like a mat, [2] though Ben and Yu were foot soldiers and Taigong were general, still I fear it would not suffice to withstand them.
translations.idiomatic: If strong leaders rally and sweep eastward, [2] even with warriors like Meng Ben and Xia Yu in the ranks and a commander like the Taigong, we might not hold them.”

sentences.zh: 議者喻以補衣猶有所完，詡恐其疽食侵淫而無限極。

translations.literal: Those who deliberate compare it to patching a garment and still having something whole; I fear it will be like a spreading ulcer eating without limit.
translations.idiomatic: They compare this to patching a coat—mine is the fear of an ulcer that spreads without end.”

sentences.zh: □之非計。

translations.literal: To [discard] it is not a plan.
translations.idiomatic: Abandoning Liang is no strategy.”

sentences.zh: 」[三]修曰：「吾意不及此。

translations.literal: " [3] Xiu said: "My thought did not reach this.
translations.idiomatic: ” [3] Li Xiu said, “I had not thought so far.”

sentences.zh: 微子之言，幾敗國事。

translations.literal: Without your words, the state's affairs would nearly have been ruined.
translations.idiomatic: Without you I might have ruined the realm.”

sentences.zh: 然則計當安出？

translations.literal: Then what plan should issue forth?
translations.idiomatic: What do you advise?”

sentences.zh: 」詡曰：「今涼土擾動，人情不安，竊憂卒然有非常之變。

translations.literal: " Xu said: "Now Liang's soil is disturbed and people's hearts are not settled; I privately worry that suddenly there may be extraordinary change.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu replied, “Liang is unsettled and the people uneasy; I fear a sudden crisis.”

sentences.zh: 誠宜令四府九卿，[四]各辟彼州數人，其牧守令長子弟皆除為□官，[五]外以勸厲，荅其功勤，內以拘致；

translations.literal: It is truly fitting to order the Four Offices and Nine Ministers [4] each to summon several men from that province, and the sons and younger brothers of its shepherds, guardians, magistrates, and chiefs all to be appointed [dispersed] officials [5]—outwardly thereby encouraging them, rewarding their merit and toil, inwardly thereby holding them in place;
translations.idiomatic: The four chief ministries and the nine ministers [4] should each recruit several men from Liang; sons of governors and magistrates should be given posts at court [5]—to honor their service abroad and keep their families here as hostages.

sentences.zh: 防其邪計。

translations.literal: and guard against their evil plots.
translations.idiomatic: That blocks treacherous designs.

sentences.zh: 」修善其言，更集四府，皆從詡議。

translations.literal: " Xiu approved his words, again assembled the Four Offices, and all followed Xu's proposal.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xiu endorsed this, reconvened the four offices, and they adopted Yu Xu’s plan.

sentences.zh: 於是辟西州豪桀為掾屬，拜牧守長吏子弟為郎，以安慰之。

translations.literal: Thereupon they summoned the bold and outstanding of the western province as staff members, and appointed the sons and younger brothers of shepherds, guardians, and senior magistrates as Gentlemen, in order to comfort them.
translations.idiomatic: They recruited western notables as aides and gave gentlemen’s appointments to officials’ sons to reassure the region.

sentences.zh: 注[一]說文曰：「喭，傳言也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The Shuowen says: "Yan means transmitted sayings."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shuowen* defines *yan* as a saying passed down.

sentences.zh: 」前書曰：「秦、漢以來，山東出相，山西出將。」

translations.literal: " The previous book [Hanshu] says: 'Since Qin and Han, east of the mountains has produced chancellors, west of the mountains has produced generals.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Hanshu* adds: ‘Since Qin and Han, Shandong has yielded ministers and Shanxi generals.’

sentences.zh: 秦時郿白起，頻陽王翦；

translations.literal: In Qin times: Bai Qi of Mei, Wang Jian of Pinyang;
translations.idiomatic: Under the Qin: Bai Qi of Mei, Wang Jian of Pinyang;

sentences.zh: 漢興，義渠公孫賀、傅介子，成紀李廣、李蔡，上邽趙充國，狄道辛武賢：皆名將也。

translations.literal: When Han rose: Gongsun He and Fu Jiezi of Yiqu, Li Guang and Li Cai of Chenji, Zhao Chongguo of Shanggui, Xin Wuxian of Didao—all were famous generals.
translations.idiomatic: After Han’s rise: Gongsun He and Fu Jiezi of Yiqu, Li Guang and Li Cai of Chenji, Zhao Chongguo of Shanggui, Xin Wuxian of Didao—each a celebrated commander.

sentences.zh: 丞相，則蕭、曹、魏、丙、韋、平、孔、翟之類也。

translations.literal: As for chancellors, there are the likes of Xiao, Cao, Wei, Bing, Wei, Ping, Kong, and Zhai.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellors such as Xiao He, Cao Shen, Wei Xiang, Bing Ji, Wei Xian, Ping Dang, Kong Guang, and Zhai Fangjin represent the east.

sentences.zh: 注[二]席捲言無餘也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] 'Roll up like a mat' means nothing left over.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Xijuan* (mat-rolling) means leaving nothing behind.

sentences.zh: 前書曰「雲徹席捲，後無餘□」也。

translations.literal: The previous book says: 'Clouds disperse, the mat is rolled up, afterward no remainder [word missing in text].'
translations.idiomatic: The *Hanshu* quotes: ‘The clouds clear, the mat is rolled up—nothing survives after.’ (The last character is missing in the source.)

sentences.zh: 注[三]疽，癰瘡也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Ju is a suppurating sore.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Ju* is an abscess or running sore.

sentences.zh: 注[四]四府謂太傅、太尉、司徒、司空之府也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] The Four Offices means the offices of the Grand Tutor, Grand Commandant, Minister of Education, and Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The ‘four offices’ are those of the grand tutor, grand commandant, minister of education, and minister of works.

sentences.zh: 九卿謂太常、光祿、衛尉、廷尉、太僕、大鴻臚、宗正、大司農、少府等也。

translations.literal: The Nine Ministers means the Minister of Ceremonies, Director of the Retainers, Commandant of the Guards, Commandant of Justice, Grand Coachman, Grand Herald, Minister of the Imperial Clan, Grand Minister of Agriculture, Privy Treasurer, and the like.
translations.idiomatic: The nine ministers are the ministers of ceremonies, retainers, guards, justice, coachman, herald, imperial clan, agriculture, and privy treasury.

sentences.zh: 注[五]□，散也，音人勇反。

translations.literal: Comment [5] [Character missing in text] means dispersed; pronunciation ren-yong fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The glossed word means ‘dispersed’; reading *reng* (ren-yong fan).

sentences.zh: 鄧騭兄弟以詡異其議，因此不平，欲以吏法中傷詡。

translations.literal: Deng Zhi's brothers, because Xu differed from their deliberation, on this account were displeased and wished to injure Xu through clerk law.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Zhi’s brothers resented Yu Xu’s dissent and looked for a legal pretext to ruin him.

sentences.zh: 後朝歌賊寧季等數千人攻殺長吏，屯聚連年，州郡不能禁，乃以詡為朝歌長。

translations.literal: Later the Chaoge bandits Ning Ji and others, several thousand men, attacked and killed senior officials, gathered and camped for successive years, and the province and commandery could not stop them; therefore Xu was made magistrate of Chaoge.
translations.idiomatic: When thousands under Ning Ji of Chaoge murdered officials and the locality could not suppress them for years, Yu Xu was named magistrate of Chaoge.

sentences.zh: 故舊皆吊詡曰：「得朝歌何衰！

translations.literal: Old associates all condoled with Xu, saying: "To get Chaoge—how unfortunate!
translations.idiomatic: Friends commiserated: “Chaoge—what bad luck!”

sentences.zh: 」詡笑曰：「志不求易，事不避難，臣之職也。

translations.literal: " Xu smiled and said: "The aim does not seek what is easy; affairs do not avoid difficulty—this is a subject's duty.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu laughed. “An official does not choose easy goals or shrink from hard duty.”

sentences.zh: 不遇盤根錯節，何以別利器乎？

translations.literal: If one does not encounter tangled roots and interlocking joints, how can one distinguish a sharp tool?
translations.idiomatic: Without gnarled roots, how do you test a blade?”

sentences.zh: 」始到，謁河內大守馬稜。

translations.literal: " When he first arrived, he visited Administrator Ma Ling of Henei.
translations.idiomatic: On arrival he called on Ma Ling, governor of Henei.

sentences.zh: [一]稜勉之曰：「君儒者，當謀謨廟堂，反在朝歌邪？

translations.literal: [1] Ling encouraged him, saying: "You are a scholar; you ought to plan strategies in the hall of the court—why instead are you in Chaoge?
translations.idiomatic: Ma Ling tried to buck him up: “You are a scholar—you belong at court planning policy, not in Chaoge.”

sentences.zh: 」詡曰：「初除之日，士大夫皆見吊勉。

translations.literal: " Xu said: "On the day of first appointment, gentlemen all came to condole and encourage.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu said, “When the appointment came, everyone offered condolences.”

sentences.zh: 以詡譸之，知其無能為也。

translations.literal: By sizing Xu up they meant to take his measure; they knew he could accomplish nothing.
translations.idiomatic: They were taking my measure—they assumed I would fail.”

sentences.zh: [二] 朝歌者，韓、魏之郊，[三]背太行，臨黃河，去敖倉百里，[四]而青、冀之人流亡萬數。

translations.literal: [2] Chaoge is the borderland of Han and Wei, [3] backs on Mount Taihang, faces the Yellow River, is one hundred li from Aocang, [4] and refugees from Qing and Ji number in the tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Chaoge lies between old Han and Wei, [3] with Taihang at its back and the Yellow River before it, [4] a hundred li from the Aocang granaries, and tens of thousands of displaced people from Qing and Ji in the area.”

sentences.zh: 賊不知開倉招觿，劫庫兵，守城戲，斷天下右臂，[五]此不足憂也。

translations.literal: The bandits do not know to open the granaries to recruit troops, seize the armory weapons, hold the city forts, and sever the right arm of the realm—[5] this is not worth worrying about.
translations.idiomatic: The rebels have not seized the granaries, armed themselves from the arsenal, or held the city walls—they have not cut off the empire’s right arm. [5] They are nothing to fear.

sentences.zh: 今其觿新盛，難與爭鋒。

translations.literal: Now their hosts are newly strong; it is hard to contest their edge.
translations.idiomatic: Their forces are at full strength; we cannot meet them head-on.

sentences.zh: 兵不猒權，願寬假轡策，勿令有所拘閡而已。

translations.literal: Troops do not reject expediency; I wish you would loosen the reins and bit and only not order anything obstructive.
translations.idiomatic: War allows stratagem—only grant me a free hand and do not tie me down.”

sentences.zh: 」[六] 及到官，設令三科以募求壯士，自掾史以下各舉所知，其攻劫者為上，傷人偷盜者次之，帶喪服而不事家業為下。

translations.literal: " [6] When he reached office, he issued orders in three categories to recruit stalwart men; from clerks and assistants downward each recommended those they knew—those who robbed with violence ranked highest, those who wounded people or stole came next, those who wore mourning garb but did not attend to family business ranked lowest.
translations.idiomatic: ” [6] Once in office he issued three classes of recruitment: clerks nominated candidates—robbers and raiders first, then assaulters and thieves, last those in mourning who neglected their households.

sentences.zh: 收得百餘人，詡為饗會，悉貰其罪，使入賊中，誘令劫掠，乃伏兵以待之，遂殺賊數百人。

translations.literal: He collected more than a hundred men; Xu held a feast for them, entirely pardoned their crimes, sent them into the bandits' midst to induce them to raid and plunder, then laid ambush soldiers to await them, and so killed several hundred bandits.
translations.idiomatic: He gathered over a hundred such men, feasted them, remitted their sentences, and sent them to lure the bandits out—then ambushed them and killed hundreds.

sentences.zh: 又潛遣貧人能縫者，傭作賊衣，以采綖縫其裾為幟，[七]有出巿裡者，吏輒禽之。

translations.literal: He also secretly sent poor people who could sew to work as hired labor sewing bandit clothing, and with colored thread sewed their hems as marks [7]; anyone who came out into the market wards, clerks immediately seized.
translations.idiomatic: He also sent poor seamstresses to sew the rebels’ clothes, stitching colored thread in the hems as a secret mark [7]; anyone who appeared in the market was arrested.

sentences.zh: 賊由是駭散，咸稱神明。

translations.literal: The bandits on this account were frightened and scattered; all called him spirit-like and enlightened.
translations.idiomatic: The bandits broke and fled; the people called him uncanny.

sentences.zh: 遷懷令。

translations.literal: He was transferred to be magistrate of Huai.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to magistrate of Huai.

sentences.zh: 注[一]稜字伯威，援族孫也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Ling's style is Bowen; he was a collateral descendant of Yuan [Ma Yuan].
translations.idiomatic: Note: Ma Ling’s courtesy name was Bowen; he was a collateral descendant of Ma Yuan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]譸當作「籌」也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Zhou should be read as 'chou' [calculate].
translations.idiomatic: Note: The character *zhou* should read *chou* (‘to reckon’).

sentences.zh: 注[三]韓界上黨，魏界河內，相接犬牙，故雲郊也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Han bordered Shangdang, Wei bordered Henei; they adjoined like dogs' teeth, hence it is called borderland.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Han met Shangdang; Wei met Henei—the frontiers interlocked, hence ‘borderland.’

sentences.zh: 注[四]敖倉在滎陽，解具安紀也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Aocang is at Xingyang; explanation is fully in the Annals of An [Emperor An].
translations.idiomatic: Note: Aocang lay at Xingyang; see the annals of Emperor An for detail.

sentences.zh: 注[五]右臂，喻要便也。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Right arm is a metaphor for strategic convenience.
translations.idiomatic: Note: ‘Right arm’ means a strategic choke point.

sentences.zh: 注[六]閡與「礙」同。

translations.literal: Comment [6] Ai is the same as 'obstruction.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The glossed word *ai* means "obstruction," homophonous with the usual graph for "hinder."

sentences.zh: 注[七]幟，記也。

translations.literal: Comment [7] Zhi means mark.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhi* is a marker or tally.

sentences.zh: 續漢書曰「以絳縷縫其裾」也。

translations.literal: The Continued Book of Han says 'with crimson thread sewed their hems.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Xu Han shu* reads: ‘They sewed crimson thread into the hems.’

sentences.zh: 後羌寇武都，鄧太后以詡有將帥之略，遷武都太守，引見嘉德殿，厚加賞賜。

translations.literal: Later when the Qiang raided Wudu, Empress Dowager Deng, because Xu had the strategy of a commander, transferred him to be Administrator of Wudu, summoned him for audience in the Jiade Hall, and richly rewarded him.
translations.idiomatic: When the Qiang struck Wudu, Empress Dowager Deng, impressed by Yu Xu’s generalship, made him governor of Wudu, received him in the Jiade Hall, and rewarded him generously.

sentences.zh: 羌乃率觿數千，遮詡於陳食、崤谷，詡即停軍不進，而宣言上書請兵，須到當發。

translations.literal: The Qiang then led several thousand troops and intercepted Xu at Canshi and Yaogu; Xu immediately halted the army and did not advance, but publicly declared he had submitted a memorial requesting troops and would set out only when they arrived.
translations.idiomatic: The Qiang mustered thousands and blocked him near Canshi and Yaogu Pass; Yu Xu halted and announced he was waiting for reinforcements he had requested before marching.

sentences.zh: 羌聞之，乃分鈔傍縣，詡因其兵散，日夜進道，兼行百餘里。

translations.literal: When the Qiang heard this, they divided to plunder neighboring counties; Xu took advantage of their forces scattering, day and night advanced on the road, and in combined marches went more than one hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: The Qiang dispersed to raid neighboring counties; Yu Xu drove his men day and night and covered over a hundred li at forced marches.

sentences.zh: 令吏士各作兩醋，日增倍之，羌不敢逼。

translations.literal: He ordered officers and soldiers each to make two cooking vessels [cu], daily doubling them; the Qiang did not dare press close.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered each soldier to carry two camp kettles and doubled the apparent number daily; the Qiang hesitated to engage, expecting reinforcements.

sentences.zh: 或問曰：「孫臏減醋而君增之。

translations.literal: Someone asked: "Sun Bin reduced the cooking vessels [cu] but you increase them.
translations.idiomatic: Someone asked: “Sun Bin reduced the number of mess-kits day by day; you increase yours.”

sentences.zh: [一]兵法日行不過三十里，以戒不虞，[二]而今日且二百里。

translations.literal: [1] By military method one does not march more than thirty li in a day, to guard against the unforeseen—[2] yet today it is nearly two hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Doctrine says thirty li a day to stay ready for surprise—[2] yet we are doing nearly two hundred.

sentences.zh: 何也？

translations.literal: Why is this?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 」詡曰：「虜觿多，吾兵少。

translations.literal: " Xu said: "The enemy's hosts are many; our troops are few.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu replied: "They outnumber us."

sentences.zh: 徐行則易為所及，速進則彼所不測。

translations.literal: If we go slowly we are easily overtaken; if we advance swiftly they cannot fathom us.
translations.idiomatic: A slow march invites pursuit; speed leaves them guessing.

sentences.zh: 虜見吾醋日增，必謂郡兵來迎。

translations.literal: When the enemy sees our cooking vessels [cu] daily increase, they will surely say commandery troops have come to meet us.
translations.idiomatic: When they see more kettles each day they will think county levies are joining us.

sentences.zh: 觿多行速，必憚追我。

translations.literal: Their hosts are many and travel swiftly—they will surely fear pursuing me.
translations.idiomatic: They are numerous but moving fast—they will not dare chase us.

sentences.zh: 孫臏見弱，吾今示強，埶有不同故也。

translations.literal: Sun Bin displayed weakness; I now display strength—the circumstances are different for this reason.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Bin feigned weakness; I feign strength—the situations differ.

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]孫臏為齊軍將，與魏龐涓戰，使齊軍入魏地，為十萬醋，明日為五萬醋，明日為三萬醋。

translations.literal: " Comment [1] Sun Bin was general of the Qi army; warring with Wei he fought Pang Juan, had the Qi army enter Wei territory, and [displayed] one hundred thousand cooking vessels [cu], the next day fifty thousand cooking vessels, the next day thirty thousand cooking vessels.
translations.idiomatic: ” Comment: Sun Bin commanded Qi against Wei’s Pang Juan and made his army appear to shrink—one day a hundred thousand kettles, the next fifty thousand, the next thirty thousand.

sentences.zh: 龐涓行三日，大喜曰：「我固知齊卒怯。

translations.literal: Pang Juan marched three days, greatly rejoiced, and said: "I long knew the Qi soldiers were timid.
translations.idiomatic: After three days Pang Juan exulted: “I knew the Qi troops were cowards.

sentences.zh: 入吾地三日，士卒亡過半矣。

translations.literal: They have entered our territory three days, and the soldiers are more than half lost."
translations.idiomatic: They have been in our land three days and more than half have deserted.”

sentences.zh: 」事見史記。

translations.literal: " The matter is seen in the Records of the Grand Historian.
translations.idiomatic: The story is in the *Shiji*.

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書王吉上疏曰：「古者師行三十里，吉行五十里。」

translations.literal: Comment [2] The previous book: Wang Ji submitted a memorial saying: 'In ancient times the army marched thirty li, good march fifty li.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Hanshu* cites Wang Ji’s memorial—armies once marched thirty li a day, his fifty.

sentences.zh: 既到郡，兵不滿三千，而羌觿萬餘，攻圍赤亭數十日。

translations.literal: When he had reached the commandery, his troops were fewer than three thousand, while the Qiang hosts were more than ten thousand; they attacked and besieged Chiting for several tens of days.
translations.idiomatic: He reached the commandery with fewer than three thousand men; over ten thousand Qiang had besieged Chiting for many days.

sentences.zh: [一]詡乃令軍中，使強弩勿發，而潛發小弩。

translations.literal: [1] Xu then ordered the army: have the strong crossbows not fire, but secretly fire the small crossbows.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Yu Xu ordered his men to hold the heavy crossbows in reserve and shoot only the light ones.

sentences.zh: 羌以為矢力弱，不能至，並兵急攻。

translations.literal: The Qiang thought the arrow power was weak and could not reach; they massed troops and pressed the attack hard.
translations.idiomatic: The Qiang assumed the bolts were too weak to reach them and launched a full assault.

sentences.zh: 詡於是使二十強弩共射一人，發無不中，羌大震，退。

translations.literal: Xu thereupon had twenty strong crossbowmen each shoot at one man; every shot hit, and the Qiang were greatly shaken and withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: He then had twenty crossbowmen concentrate on single targets—every bolt hit—and the Qiang broke and pulled back.

sentences.zh: 詡因出城奮擊，多所傷殺。

translations.literal: Xu therefore went out of the city and struck fiercely, wounding and killing many.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu sallied from the city and cut down many of them.

sentences.zh: 明日悉陳其兵觿，令從東郭門出，北[二]郭門入，貿易衣服，回轉數周。

translations.literal: The next day he fully displayed his troop hosts, ordered them to go out the eastern outer gate and enter the northern [2] outer gate, exchange clothing, and circle round several times.
translations.idiomatic: The next day he paraded his whole force out the east gate, back in through the north gate [2], had them swap coats, and marched the column around several times.

sentences.zh: 羌不知其數，更相恐動。

translations.literal: The Qiang did not know their number and mutually frightened one another.
translations.idiomatic: Unable to count his men, the Qiang panicked among themselves.

sentences.zh: 詡計賊當退，乃潛遣五百餘人於淺水設伏，候其走路。

translations.literal: Xu calculated the bandits ought to retreat; he secretly sent more than five hundred men to lay ambush in shallow water and wait on their escape route.
translations.idiomatic: Expecting a retreat, he hid five hundred men in the shallows along their line of flight.

sentences.zh: 虜果大奔，因掩擊，大破之，斬獲甚觿，賊由是敗散，南入益州。

translations.literal: The enemy indeed fled in great disorder; he took advantage to strike from concealment, greatly defeated them, beheaded and captured very many; the bandits on this account were defeated and scattered, fleeing south into Yizhou.
translations.idiomatic: They fled in disorder; Yu Xu ambushed them, won a crushing victory, and took many heads and prisoners. The rebels scattered south into Yi Province.

sentences.zh: 詡乃占相地埶，築營壁百八十所，招還流亡，假賑貧人，郡遂以安。

translations.literal: Xu then surveyed the terrain, built camp ramparts at one hundred eighty places, summoned back the displaced, lent relief to the poor, and the commandery thereupon was settled.
translations.idiomatic: He surveyed the ground, threw up a hundred eighty outposts, brought refugees home, fed the destitute, and restored calm to the commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[一]赤亭故城在今渭州襄武縣東南，有赤亭水也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The old city of Chiting is southeast of Xiangwu County in modern Weizhou; there is Chiting River.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Old Chiting lay southeast of present-day Xiangwu in Weizhou, beside the Chiting River.

sentences.zh: 注[二]一作「西」。

translations.literal: Comment [2] One text reads "west."
translations.idiomatic: Note: Another edition reads “west” instead of “north.”

sentences.zh: 先是運道艱險，舟車不通，驢馬負載，僦五致一。

translations.literal: Previously the transport route was perilous and steep; boats and carts could not pass; donkeys and horses bore loads—hire five to deliver one.
translations.idiomatic: Supply lines had been treacherous: where carts and boats failed, pack animals carried freight at ruinous cost—five loads paid for one delivered.

sentences.zh: [一]詡乃自將吏士，案行川谷，自沮至下辯[二]數十里中，皆燒石翦木，開漕船道，[三]以人僦直雇借傭者，於是水運通利，歲省四千餘萬。

translations.literal: [1] Xu then personally led officers and soldiers, inspected the river valleys, from Ju down to Xia Bian [2] across several tens of li, all burning stone and cutting wood, opening a canal for boats [3]; he used hired wages to employ borrowers and laborers, and thereby water transport became convenient; yearly savings exceeded forty million.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He led his men along the valley from Ju to Xia Bian [2], blasted rock and cut timber, and opened a navigable channel [3], paying laborers a fair wage. River transport improved and the commandery saved over forty million cash a year.

sentences.zh: 詡始到郡，戶裁盈萬。

translations.literal: When Xu first reached the commandery, households barely exceeded ten thousand.
translations.idiomatic: When he arrived, the register listed barely ten thousand households.

sentences.zh: 及綏聚荒余，招還流散，二三年閒，遂增至四萬餘戶。

translations.literal: And when he had soothed and gathered the wasteland remainder and summoned back the scattered wanderers, within two or three years it gradually increased to more than forty thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Within two or three years of resettling the countryside and recalling exiles, households rose above forty thousand.

sentences.zh: 鹽米豐賤，十倍於前。

translations.literal: Salt and rice were abundant and cheap—ten times compared to before.
translations.idiomatic: Salt and grain became cheap—ten times cheaper than before.

sentences.zh: [四]坐法免。

translations.literal: [4] He sat guilty by law and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: [4] He was later removed from office for a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: 注[一]廣雅曰：「僦，賃也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The Guangya says: "Jiu means hire."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Guangya* glosses *jiu* as “hire.”

sentences.zh: 」音子救反。

translations.literal: Pronunciation zi-jiu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading: *zi-jiu* fan.

sentences.zh: 僦五致一謂用五石賃而致一石也。

translations.literal: "Hire five to deliver one" means using five dan of hire fee to deliver one dan.
translations.idiomatic: “Five for one” meant paying five *dan* in transport costs to move one *dan* of grain.

sentences.zh: 注[二]沮及下辯並縣名。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Ju and Xia Bian are both county names.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Ju and Xia Bian were both county seats.

sentences.zh: 沮，今興州順政縣也。

translations.literal: Ju is modern Xingzhou Shunzheng County.
translations.idiomatic: Ju is present-day Shunzheng in Xingzhou.

sentences.zh: 下辯，今成州同谷縣也。

translations.literal: Xia Bian is modern Chengzhou Tonggu County.
translations.idiomatic: Xia Bian is present-day Tonggu in Chengzhou.

sentences.zh: 沮音七余反。

translations.literal: Ju pronunciation qi-yu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading: *qi-yu* fan (the place-name 沮).

sentences.zh: 注[三]續漢書曰「下辯東三十餘里有峽，中當泉水，生大石，障塞水流，每至春夏，輒溢沒秋稼，壞敗營郭。

translations.literal: Comment [3] The Continued Book of Han says: "East of Xia Bian more than thirty li there is a gorge; amid it is spring water, and a great rock was born, blocking and damming the flow; each time it reached spring and summer it always overflowed and drowned autumn crops, ruining the camps and outer walls.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Xu Han shu* describes a gorge east of Xia Bian where a boulder dammed the stream, flooding fields each spring.

sentences.zh: 詡乃使人燒石，以水灌之，石皆坼裂，因鐫去石，遂無泛溺之患」也。

translations.literal: Xu then had people burn the rock, pour water on it, and the rocks all split open; they therefore chiseled away the rock, and thereupon there was no calamity of flooding."
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu fired the stone, poured water on it to crack it, and removed the debris—ending the floods.

sentences.zh: 注[四]續漢書曰：「詡始到，谷石千，鹽石八千，見戶萬三千。

translations.literal: Comment [4] The Continued Book of Han says: "When Xu first arrived, grain was one thousand cash per dan, salt eight thousand per dan; registered households were thirteen thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Xu Han shu* records that on his arrival grain cost a thousand and salt eight thousand per *dan*, with thirteen thousand households.

sentences.zh: 視事三歲，米石八十，鹽石四百，流人還歸，郡戶數萬，人足家給，一郡無事」。

translations.literal: After three years in office, grain was eighty cash per dan, salt four hundred per dan; wandering people returned home, commandery households numbered in the tens of thousands, people sufficient and families supplied, and the whole commandery had no troubles."
translations.idiomatic: After three years grain fell to eighty cash and salt to four hundred; refugees returned; households numbered in the tens of thousands; the commandery was untroubled.

sentences.zh: 注[二]韓詩外傳曰「昔者衛大夫史魚病且死，謂其子曰：『我數言蘧伯玉之賢而不能進，彌子瑕不肖不能退。

translations.literal: Comment [2] The Han Outside Commentary to the Classic of Songs says: "Formerly the Wei grandee Shi Yu was ill and near death; he said to his son: 'I repeatedly spoke of Qu Boyu's worthiness but could not advance him; Mizi Xia was unworthy but I could not dismiss him.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Han shi waizhuan* quotes Minister Shi Yu of Wei on his deathbed: “I praised Qu Boyu’s virtue yet failed to promote him; I knew Mizi Xia was base yet failed to dismiss him.

sentences.zh: 為人臣生不能進賢而退不肖，死不當理喪正堂，殯我於室足矣。

translations.literal: As a subject, if in life I cannot advance the worthy and dismiss the unworthy, in death I ought not be laid out in the main hall—bury me in the chamber and it suffices.'
translations.idiomatic: As a minister who failed to elevate the good and remove the bad, I should not lie in the main hall—bury me in the side chamber.”

sentences.zh: 』衛君問其故，子以父言聞，君乃立召蘧伯玉而貴之，彌子瑕而退之，徙殯於正堂，成禮而後去」也。

translations.literal: The ruler of Wei asked the reason; the son reported the father's words; the ruler thereupon immediately summoned Qu Boyu and honored him, dismissed Mizi Xia, moved the bier to the main hall, completed the rites and afterward departed."
translations.idiomatic: The Wei lord asked why; the son repeated his father’s words. The ruler summoned Qu Boyu and honored him, banished Mizi Xia, moved the coffin to the main hall, and finished the rites.

sentences.zh: 注[三]漢官儀曰：「敦字文理，京*

translations.literal: Comment [3] Han Official Regulations says: "Dun's style is Wenli, Jing
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Han guan yi* gives Wang Dun’s courtesy name as Wenli and his origin as Jing

translations.literal: zhao)
translations.idiomatic: zhao)

translations.literal: County man."
translations.idiomatic: County.”

sentences.zh: 時中常侍張防特用權埶，每請托受取，詡輒案之，而屢寑不報。

translations.literal: At that time the Palace Regular Attendant Zhang Fang especially wielded power; each time he requested favors and took bribes; Xu always investigated him, but repeatedly the reports lay unanswered.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuch Zhang Fang wielded great influence, soliciting favors and taking bribes; Yu Xu repeatedly impeached him, but the court buried each memorial.

sentences.zh: 詡不勝其憤，乃自系廷尉，奏言曰：「昔孝安皇帝任用樊豐，遂交亂嫡統，幾亡社稷。

translations.literal: Xu, unable to bear his anger, bound himself and went to the Commandant of Justice, submitting a memorial saying: "Formerly Emperor Xiaoan employed Fan Feng, and thereby the legitimate succession was thrown into chaos; the altars nearly perished.
translations.idiomatic: Beside himself, Yu Xu delivered himself to the Commandant of Justice and wrote: “Emperor An employed Fan Feng and nearly lost the throne.

sentences.zh: 今者張防復弄威柄，國家之禍將重至矣。

translations.literal: Now Zhang Fang again toys with the handle of authority; the state's calamity will arrive again heavily.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang now abuses power again—the disaster will repeat.”

sentences.zh: 臣不忍與防同朝，謹自系以聞，無令臣襲楊震之跡。

translations.literal: Your subject cannot bear to share court with Fang; I respectfully bind myself to report—do not let your subject follow in Yang Zhen's footsteps."
translations.idiomatic: I cannot serve in the same court with him—I surrender myself lest I share Yang Zhen’s fate.”

sentences.zh: 」[一]書奏，防流涕訴帝，詡坐論輸左校。

translations.literal: " [1] When the memorial was submitted, Fang wept and complained to the emperor; Xu sat convicted and was sentenced to labor in the Left Corps.
translations.idiomatic: ” [1] Zhang Fang wept to the emperor; Yu Xu was sentenced to corvée in the Left Construction Corps.

sentences.zh: 防必欲害之，二日之中，傳考四獄。

translations.literal: Fang was determined to harm him; within two days he was transferred and interrogated in four prisons.
translations.idiomatic: Fang meant to destroy him: within two days he was hauled through four jails for interrogation.

sentences.zh: 獄吏勸詡自引，詡曰：「寧伏歐刀以示遠近。

translations.literal: The prison clerks urged Xu to confess himself; Xu said: "I would rather submit to the executioner's blade to show those far and near."
translations.idiomatic: Jailers urged a confession; Yu Xu said, “I would rather die under the headsman’s blade in full view of the realm.”

sentences.zh: 」[二]宦者孫程、張賢等知詡以忠獲罪，乃相率奏乞見。

translations.literal: " [2] The eunuchs Sun Cheng, Zhang Xian, and others knew Xu had been convicted for loyalty, and together submitted a memorial begging audience.
translations.idiomatic: ” [2] Eunuchs Sun Cheng and Zhang Xian, knowing he had been wronged, jointly petitioned for an audience.

sentences.zh: 程曰：「陛下始與臣等造事之時，[三]常疾奸臣，知其傾國。

translations.literal: Cheng said: "When Your Majesty first made affairs with us subjects [3], you constantly hated treacherous ministers and knew they overturn states.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Cheng said, “When we raised you to the throne [3], you hated corrupt ministers as ruinous to the state.

sentences.zh: 今者即位而復自為，何以非先帝乎？

translations.literal: Now that you have mounted the throne you again do it yourself—how can you condemn the former emperor?
translations.idiomatic: Now you repeat their crimes—how does that differ from what you condemned in the late emperor?”

sentences.zh: 司隸校尉虞詡為陛下盡忠，而更被拘繫；

translations.literal: Colonel Director of Retainers Yu Xu is utterly loyal to Your Majesty, yet is instead bound and imprisoned;
translations.idiomatic: Colonel of the Retainers Yu Xu has served you faithfully, yet he sits in chains;

sentences.zh: 常侍張防臧罪明正，反構忠良。

translations.literal: Regular Attendant Zhang Fang's crimes of corruption are clear and correct, yet he inversely frames the loyal and good.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang’s guilt is plain, yet he slanders the loyal.

sentences.zh: 今客星守羽林，其占宮中有奸臣。

translations.literal: Now a guest star guards Yulin; its omen is that there is a treacherous minister within the palace.
translations.idiomatic: A stray star stands over Yulin—the omen points to a traitor inside the palace.

sentences.zh: [四]宜急收防送獄，以塞天變。

translations.literal: [4] You should urgently seize Fang and send him to prison, to stop Heaven's anomaly.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Arrest Zhang Fang at once and pacify heaven’s warning.”

sentences.zh: 下詔出詡，還假印綬。

translations.literal: An edict issued Xu out and returned his seals and ribbons on temporary authority.
translations.idiomatic: An edict freed Yu Xu and restored his credentials.

sentences.zh: 」時防立在帝后，程乃叱防曰：「奸臣張防，何不下殿！

translations.literal: " At that time Fang stood behind the emperor; Cheng then shouted at Fang: "Treacherous minister Zhang Fang—why do you not descend the hall!
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang stood behind the throne; Sun Cheng shouted, “You, traitor—get off the dais!”

sentences.zh: 」防不得已，趨就東箱。

translations.literal: " Fang had no choice but to hurry to the eastern gallery.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang slunk to the east gallery.

sentences.zh: [五]程曰：「陛下急收防，無令從阿母求請。」

translations.literal: [5] Cheng said: "Your Majesty urgently seize Fang; do not let him seek pardon from the nurse."
translations.idiomatic: [5] “Seize him now—do not let him beg your nurse for mercy.”

sentences.zh: [六]帝問諸尚書，尚書賈朗素與防善，證詡之罪。

translations.literal: [6] The emperor questioned the Masters of Writing; Master of Writing Jia Lang had always been friendly with Fang and testified to Xu's crime.
translations.idiomatic: [6] The emperor asked the Masters of Writing; Jia Lang, Zhang Fang’s ally, testified against Yu Xu.

sentences.zh: 帝疑焉，謂程曰：「且出，吾方思之。

translations.literal: The emperor was doubtful and said to Cheng: "For now go out; I am just considering it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor hesitated: “Withdraw for now—I need to think.”

sentences.zh: 」於是詡子顗與門生百餘人，舉幡候中常侍高梵車，叩頭流血，訴言枉狀。

translations.literal: " Thereupon Xu's son Yi with more than a hundred disciples raised banners and waited for Regular Attendant Gao Fan's carriage, knocked their heads until bleeding, and complained of the injustice.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Yi led a hundred disciples to block Eunuch Gao Fan’s carriage, bloodying their foreheads with appeals of injustice.

sentences.zh: 梵乃入言之，防坐徙邊，賈朗等六人或死或黜，即日赦出詡。

translations.literal: Fan then entered and spoke; Fang sat convicted and was banished to the frontier; Jia Lang and six others either died or were degraded; that same day an amnesty issued Xu out.
translations.idiomatic: Gao Fan spoke for them: Zhang Fang was exiled, Jia Lang and six others died or were cashiered, and Yu Xu was freed that day.

sentences.zh: 程復上書陳詡有大功，語甚切激。

translations.literal: Cheng again submitted a memorial stating Xu had great merit; the language was very cutting and impassioned.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Cheng memorialized again, praising Yu Xu’s service in fierce terms.

sentences.zh: 帝感悟，復征拜議郎。

translations.literal: The emperor was moved and understood; again summoned and appointed Consultant.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor relented and recalled him as a consultant.

sentences.zh: 數日，遷尚書僕射。

translations.literal: Within several days he was transferred to Vice Director of the Masters of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: Within days he became vice director of the Masters of Writing.

sentences.zh: 注[一]震為樊豐所譖而死。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Zhen was slandered by Fan Feng and died.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yang Zhen died after Fan Feng’s slander.

sentences.zh: 注[二]歐刀，刑人之刀也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Ou blade is the executioner's blade.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The “Ou blade” is the headsman’s sword.

sentences.zh: 注[三]謂順帝為太子，被江京等廢為濟陰王，程等謀立之時也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] It refers to when Shun Emperor was heir-apparent, was by Jiang Jing and others degraded to Prince of Jiyin, and Cheng and others plotted to establish him.
translations.idiomatic: Note: This refers to the plot to restore Emperor Shun after his deposition as heir.

sentences.zh: 注[四]史記天官書曰「虛、危南有觿星，曰羽林」也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] The Records of the Grand Historian Treatise on Heavenly Offices says: "South of Xu and Wei there is a host of stars called Yulin."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shiji* “Celestial Offices” places the Yulin asterism south of Xu and Wei.

sentences.zh: 注[五]埤蒼云：「箱，序也。

translations.literal: Comment [5] The Bicang says: "Xiang means sequence."
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Bicang* glosses *xiang* as “wing” or “side hall.”

sentences.zh: 」字或作「廂」。

translations.literal: The character sometimes is written "xiang" [wing/gallery].
translations.idiomatic: Some texts write the character as *xiang* (“gallery”).

sentences.zh: 注[六]阿母，宋娥也。

translations.literal: Comment [6] The nurse is Song E.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The “nurse” is Song E.

sentences.zh: 是時長吏、二千石聽百姓□罰者輸贖，號為「義錢」，托為貧人儲，而守令因以聚斂。

translations.literal: At that time senior officials and two-thousand-dan [officials] listened to common people [subject to] [missing character] fines paying ransom, styled "righteous cash," nominally stored for the poor, but guardians and magistrates used it to enrich themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrates allowed “voluntary” fines in lieu of punishment, called “alms money” for the poor, which officials pocketed. (The source text has a damaged character in the phrase for those fined.)

sentences.zh: 詡上疏曰：「元年以來，貧百姓章言長吏受取百萬以上者，匈匈不絕，□罰吏人至數千萬，而三公、刺史少所舉奏。

translations.literal: Xu submitted a memorial saying: "Since the first year, poor common people have petitioned in chapters that senior officials who took more than a million have been unceasingly clamorous; [missing character] fines on clerks and people reach tens of millions, yet the Three Dukes and Inspectors seldom memorialize.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu wrote: “Petitions flood in accusing officials who took millions; fines total tens of millions, yet the Three Dukes and inspectors rarely impeach anyone.”

sentences.zh: 尋永平、章和中，州郡以走卒錢給貸貧人，[一]司空劾案，州及郡縣皆坐免黜。

translations.literal: Seeking the Yongping and Zhanghe periods, commanderies and prefectures used runner cash to lend to the poor; [1] the Minister of Works impeached and investigated, and the province and commanderies and counties all sat dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongping and Zhanghe similar “runner money” schemes drew impeachment from the minister of works and mass dismissals.

sentences.zh: 今宜遵前典，蠲除權制。

translations.literal: Now it is fitting to follow the former canons and abolish expedient measures.
translations.idiomatic: We should follow those precedents and abolish these abuses.”

sentences.zh: 」於是詔書下詡章，切責州郡。

translations.literal: " Thereupon an edict copied Xu's memorial and sternly rebuked the provinces and commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: An edict circulated his memorial and rebuked every province and commandery.

sentences.zh: □罰輸贖自此而止。

translations.literal: [Missing character] fines paying ransom from this stopped.
translations.idiomatic: The practice of buying off punishments ended.

sentences.zh: 注[一]走卒，伍伯之類也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Runners are of the class of village elders.
translations.idiomatic: Note: “Runners” here means bailiffs such as village elders.

sentences.zh: 續漢志曰：「伍伯，公八人，中二千石六人，千石、六百石皆四人，自*[四]*百石以下至二百石皆二人。

translations.literal: The Continued Han Treatise says: "Wu bo: dukes eight men, fully two-thousand-dan six men, one-thousand-dan and six-hundred-dan all four men, from four-hundred-dan down to two-hundred-dan all two men.
translations.idiomatic: The *Xu Han zhi* lists runners (*wu bo*) allotted by rank—eight for dukes down to two for lower salaries.

sentences.zh: 黃綬。

translations.literal: Yellow ribbons.
translations.idiomatic: Yellow ribbons.

sentences.zh: 武官伍伯，文官辟車。

translations.literal: Military officials have wu bo; civil officials have pi-che [carriage clearers].
translations.idiomatic: Military officers used runners; civil officers used carriage escorts.

sentences.zh: 鈴下、侍閣、門蘭、部署、街*[裡]*走卒，皆有程品，多少隨所典領，率皆赤幘縫□。

translations.literal: Bell attendants, chamber attendants, gate orchids, deployment, street *[ward]* runners—all have regulated grades; quantity follows what they oversee and lead; generally all wear red kerchiefs sewn [missing character].
translations.idiomatic: Grooms, ushers, gate guards, and street runners were graded by duty—mostly in red kerchiefs with colored trim (text damaged).

sentences.zh: 」即今行鞭杖者也。

translations.literal: " These are those who today conduct flogging and clubbing.
translations.idiomatic: These were the men who enforced floggings.

sentences.zh: 此言錢者，令其出資錢，不役其身也。

translations.literal: As for this speaking of cash, it ordered them to contribute cash funds and not labor their persons.
translations.idiomatic: The “cash” meant paying money instead of labor.

sentences.zh: 先是寧陽主簿詣闕，訴其縣令之枉，[一]積六七歲不省。

translations.literal: Previously the Ningyang chief clerk went to the palace gate and complained of his county magistrate's injustice; [1] for six or seven years accumulated it was not examined.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier a Ningyang clerk petitioned about a corrupt magistrate [1]; six or seven years passed with no response.

sentences.zh: 主簿乃上書曰：「臣為陛下子，陛下為臣父。

translations.literal: The chief clerk then submitted a memorial saying: "Your subject is Your Majesty's son; Your Majesty is Your subject's father.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote: “I am your son; you are my father.

sentences.zh: 臣章百上，終不見省，臣豈可北詣單于以告怨乎？

translations.literal: Your subject's memorial went up a hundred times and in the end was not seen; could your subject go north to the Chanyu to complain of resentment?
translations.idiomatic: I submitted a hundred petitions with no answer—must I appeal to the Xiongnu chanyu?”

sentences.zh: 」帝大怒，持章示尚書，尚書遂劾以大逆。

translations.literal: " The emperor was greatly angered, held up the memorial to show the Masters of Writing; the Masters of Writing thereupon impeached him for great treason.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor raged and showed the memorial to the Masters of Writing, who charged the clerk with treason.

sentences.zh: 詡駁之曰：「主簿所訟，乃君父之怨；

translations.literal: Xu refuted it, saying: "What the chief clerk sued over is resentment between lord and father;
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu objected: “He voiced a son’s grievance against a father figure;

sentences.zh: 百上不達，是有司之過。

translations.literal: a hundred times not reaching superiors is the fault of the responsible officials.
translations.idiomatic: failure to answer a hundred petitions is the bureaucracy’s fault.

sentences.zh: 愚惷之人，不足多誅。

translations.literal: Stupid and blunt people are not worth executing many times.
translations.idiomatic: The man is a fool—hardly worth execution.”

sentences.zh: 」帝納詡言，笞之而已。

translations.literal: " The emperor accepted Xu's words and flogged him only.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted Yu Xu’s view and had him caned.

sentences.zh: 詡因謂諸尚書曰：「小人有怨，不遠千里，斷髮刻肌，詣闕告訴，而不為理，豈臣下之義？

translations.literal: Xu therefore said to the various Masters of Writing: "When petty people have resentment, not distant a thousand li, they cut hair and carve flesh, go to the palace gate to complain and sue, and are not given handling—is this the duty of subjects?
translations.idiomatic: He told the Masters of Writing: “When commoners travel a thousand li, mutilate themselves, and appeal at the gate, ignoring them is no way to govern.

sentences.zh: 君與濁長吏何親，而與怨人何仇乎？

translations.literal: How are you close to corrupt senior officials, and how are you enemy to the resentful people?
translations.idiomatic: Why side with bad magistrates against those who seek justice?”

sentences.zh: 」聞者皆籩。

translations.literal: " Those who heard all were ashamed.
translations.idiomatic: His hearers were ashamed.

sentences.zh: 詡又上言：「台郎顯職，仕之通階。

translations.literal: Xu again submitted a statement: "Platform Gentleman is a prominent office; it is the common ladder of office.
translations.idiomatic: He also wrote: “Palace gentlemen are prestigious posts—the standard path to office.

sentences.zh: 今或一郡七八，或一州無人。

translations.literal: Now sometimes one commandery has seven or eight, sometimes one province has none.
translations.idiomatic: Some commanderies hold seven or eight such posts; some provinces hold none.

sentences.zh: 宜令均平，以厭天下之望。

translations.literal: It is fitting to order even balance, to satisfy the expectation of the realm.
translations.idiomatic: The quotas should be balanced to satisfy the empire.”

sentences.zh: 」及諸奏議，多見從用。

translations.literal: " And his various memorials and proposals were mostly followed and employed.
translations.idiomatic: Most of his memorials were adopted.

sentences.zh: 注[一]寧陽，縣，屬東平國，故城在今兗州龔丘縣南也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Ningyang is a county belonging to Dongping state; the old city is south of Gongqiu County in modern Yanzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Ningyang County lay in Dongping; its old site was south of present Gongqiu in Yanzhou.

sentences.zh: 詡好刺舉，無所回容，[一]數以此忤權戚，遂九見譴考，三遭刑罰，而剛正之性，終老不屈。

translations.literal: Xu liked to expose and impeach; he had no turning aside or tolerance; [1] several times thereby offended powerful kin; thus nine times he saw demotion and interrogation, three times suffered punishment, yet his upright and correct nature to the end in old age did not bend.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu made a habit of impeachment without pulling punches [1], repeatedly offending great families—nine investigations, three punishments—yet he never bent.

sentences.zh: 永和初，遷尚書令，以公事去官。

translations.literal: In the early Yonghe period he was transferred to Director of the Masters of Writing and dismissed office for public affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Yonghe he became director of the Masters of Writing but left office over an administrative matter.

sentences.zh: 朝廷思其忠，復征之，會卒。

translations.literal: The court thought of his loyalty and again summoned him; he happened to die.
translations.idiomatic: The court missed his loyalty and summoned him again; he died before answering.

sentences.zh: 臨終，謂其子恭曰：「吾事君直道，行己無愧，所悔者為朝歌長時殺賊數百人，其中何能不有冤者。

translations.literal: Approaching death he said to his son Gong: "I served the lord by the straight path; in conduct I have no shame; what I regret is that when I was magistrate of Chaoge I killed several hundred bandits—among them how could there not be those wrongly [killed].
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed he told his son Gong: “I served with an honest heart, yet I regret executing hundreds as magistrate of Chaoge—some must have been innocent.”

sentences.zh: 自此二十餘年，家門不增一口，斯獲罪於天也。

translations.literal: From this for twenty-some years, the household has not increased by one person—thus I have offended Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: For more than twenty years since, no child has been born to our line—I have earned Heaven’s blame.

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]回，曲也。

translations.literal: " Comment [1] Hui means bent.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Hui* here means “to bend” or “turn.”

sentences.zh: 恭有俊才，官至上黨太守。

translations.literal: Gong had outstanding talent; his office reached Administrator of Shangdang.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Gong was a man of talent and rose to governor of Shangdang.

sentences.zh: 傅燮字南容，北地靈州人也。

translations.literal: Fu Xie, styled Nanrong, was a man of Lingzhou in Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xie, courtesy name Nanrong, came from Lingzhou in Beidi commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]本字幼起，慕南容三復白珪，乃易字焉。

translations.literal: [1] Originally styled Youqi; admiring Nancrong's thrice repeating the white-jade maxim, he therefore changed his style.
translations.idiomatic: [1] His childhood name was Youqi; out of admiration for Nangong Tao’s habit of dwelling on the “flaw in the white jade,” he took Nanrong as his courtesy name.

sentences.zh: [二] 身長八尺，有威容。

translations.literal: [2] He stood eight chi tall and had an imposing bearing.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He stood eight *chi* tall and carried himself with authority.

sentences.zh: 少師事太尉劉寬。

translations.literal: In youth he studied as disciple under Grand Commandant Liu Kuan.
translations.idiomatic: As a young man he studied under Grand Commandant Liu Kuan.

sentences.zh: 再舉孝廉。

translations.literal: He was nominated Filial and Incorrupt twice.
translations.idiomatic: He was twice recommended as filial and incorrupt.

sentences.zh: 聞所舉郡將喪，乃□官行服。

translations.literal: When he heard that the commandery general who had nominated him had died, he then [left] office to observe mourning.
translations.idiomatic: When his patron who had nominated him died, he resigned his post to mourn. (The verb is damaged in the received text.)

sentences.zh: 後為護軍司馬，與左中郎*[將]*皇甫嵩俱討賊張角。

translations.literal: Later he was Colonel Protecting the Army, and with General of the Left Center Huangfu Song together campaigned against the bandit Zhang Jue.
translations.idiomatic: He later served as protector-colonel of the army alongside Huangfu Song, general of the Left Center, against Zhang Jue.

sentences.zh: 注[一]靈州，縣也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Lingzhou is a county.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Lingzhou was a county seat.

sentences.zh: 注[二]家語子貢對衛文子曰：「一日三復白珪之玷，是南宮絛之行也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] The Family Sayings: Zigong answered Wei Wenzi: "In one day thrice pondering the flaw in the white jade—that was the conduct of Nangong Tao.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Kongzi jiayu* quotes Zigong’s praise of Nangong Tao’s scruple about the “flaw in the white jade.”

sentences.zh: 」王肅注云：「玷，缺也。

translations.literal: Wang Su's commentary says: "Dian means flaw."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Su glosses *dian* as “blemish.”

sentences.zh: 詩云：『白珪之玷，尚可磨也。

translations.literal: The ode says: 'The flaw in the white jade may still be ground away.
translations.idiomatic: As the *Odes* say, “The blemish on white jade may still be polished away.

sentences.zh: 斯言之玷，不可為也。

translations.literal: The flaw in these words cannot be repaired.
translations.idiomatic: But a flaw in one’s words cannot be mended.”

sentences.zh: 』一日三復，慎之至也。」

translations.literal: ' To thrice repeat in one day is the utmost of caution."
translations.idiomatic: To rehearse it three times in a day is the height of care.”

sentences.zh: 燮素疾中官，既行，因上疏曰：「臣聞天下之禍，不由於外，皆興於內。

translations.literal: Xie always hated the palace attendants; once underway he submitted a memorial saying: "Your subject has heard that under-Heaven's calamities do not come from without—all arise from within.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xie despised the eunuchs. On campaign he memorialized: “Calamity does not come from abroad—it springs from within the court.

sentences.zh: 是故虞舜升朝，先除四凶，然後用十六相。

translations.literal: Therefore when Shun of Yu ascended to court he first removed the Four Fiends, then employed the sixteen assistants.
translations.idiomatic: That is why Shun executed the Four Fiends before he raised the sixteen worthy ministers.”

sentences.zh: [一]明惡人不去，則善人無由進也。

translations.literal: [1] It clarifies that if wicked men are not removed, good men have no means to advance.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Evil men must be cleared away before good men can rise.”

sentences.zh: 今張角起於趙、魏，黃巾亂於六州。

translations.literal: Now Zhang Jue has risen in Zhao and Wei; the Yellow Turbans riot in six provinces.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Jue has stirred Zhao and Wei; the Yellow Turbans ravage six provinces.

sentences.zh: [二]此皆釁發蕭牆，而禍延四海者也。

translations.literal: [2] All these are cracks that began within the inner screen, yet the calamity extends to the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: [2] These disorders began inside the palace walls yet engulf the realm.”

sentences.zh: 臣受戎任，奉辭伐罪，始到穎川，戰無不□。

translations.literal: Your subject received military commission, received command to punish crimes; I had just reached Yingchuan and in every battle I prevailed (text damaged; sense: undefeated).
translations.idiomatic: I took up arms at imperial order; from the day I reached Yingchuan I did not know defeat. (The text is damaged; the sense is that every engagement succeeded.)

sentences.zh: 黃巾雖盛，不足為廟堂憂也。

translations.literal: Though the Yellow Turbans flourish, they are not worth worry for the temple hall.
translations.idiomatic: The Turbans themselves are not what should frighten the court.

sentences.zh: 臣之所懼，在於治水不自其源，末流彌增其廣耳。

translations.literal: What your subject fears is this: if one dams water not at its source, the downstream flow only grows broader.
translations.idiomatic: I fear you are treating the symptom—the flood spreads farther downstream.”

sentences.zh: 陛下仁德寬容，多所不忍，故閹豎弄權，忠臣不進。

translations.literal: Your Majesty's benevolent virtue is accommodating and tolerant; much you cannot bear—therefore eunuchs toy with power and loyal ministers do not advance.
translations.idiomatic: Your mercy spares too many—eunuchs rule while loyal men stand idle.”

sentences.zh: 誠使張角梟夷，黃巾變服，臣之所憂，甫益深耳。

translations.literal: Truly if Zhang Jue were beheaded and the Yellow Turbans changed clothing, your subject's worries would only then begin to deepen.
translations.idiomatic: Execute Zhang Jue and scatter the Turbans, and my deeper fear only begins.”

sentences.zh: [三]何者？

translations.literal: [3] Why?
translations.idiomatic: [3] Why?

sentences.zh: 夫邪正之人不宜共國，亦猶冰炭不可同器。

translations.literal: Evil and upright men ought not share one state—also as ice and charcoal cannot long share one vessel.
translations.idiomatic: The wicked and the righteous cannot govern together—ice and charcoal do not stay in the same bowl.”

sentences.zh: [四]彼知正人之功顯，而危亡之兆見，皆將巧辭飾說，共長虛偽。

translations.literal: [4] When they see upright men's merit shine forth and omens of peril appear, all will frame clever excuses together and extend hypocrisy.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Once honest officers succeed, the corrupt will crowd them with lies.”

sentences.zh: 夫孝子疑於屢至，[五]巿虎成於三夫。

translations.literal: A filial son is suspected when reports come repeatedly; [5] the market tiger is completed by three men.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Even a devoted son is doubted after repeated tales—as when three rumors invent a tiger in the market.”

sentences.zh: [六]若不詳察真偽，忠臣將復有杜郵之戮矣。

translations.literal: [6] If you do not examine truth and falsehood carefully, loyal ministers will again suffer execution at Duyou.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Without judging truth from slander, loyal men will die like Bai Qi at Duyou.”

sentences.zh: [七]陛下宜思虞舜四罪之舉，速行讒佞放殛之誅，[八]則善人思進，奸凶自息。

translations.literal: [7] Your Majesty should recall Shun's banishment of the four crimes, swiftly execute slanderers and toadies or banish them, [8] then good men will wish to advance and evil will subside of itself.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Act as Shun did against the four criminals—purge flatterers at once [8] so worthies advance and villains fall silent.”

sentences.zh: 臣聞忠臣之事君，猶孝子之事父也。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that a loyal minister's serving his lord is like a filial son's serving his father.
translations.idiomatic: A loyal minister serves his sovereign as a son serves a father.

sentences.zh: 子之事父，焉得不盡其情？

translations.literal: A son serving his father—how can he not exhaust his devotion?
translations.idiomatic: How could a son hold anything back?

sentences.zh: 使臣身備鈇鉞之戮，陛下少用其言，國之福也。

translations.literal: Though your subject's person await execution by axe and halberd, if Your Majesty used a little of your subject's words—that would be the state's blessing.
translations.idiomatic: Let me die by the axe if need be—only heed part of this advice and the realm will benefit.”

sentences.zh: 」書奏，宦者趙忠見而忿惡。

translations.literal: " When the memorial went up, the eunuch Zhao Zhong saw it and was furious.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuch Zhao Zhong read it and was enraged.

sentences.zh: 及破張角，燮功多當封，忠訴譖之，[九]靈帝猶識燮言，[一０]得不加罪，竟亦不封，以為安定都尉。

translations.literal: When they had defeated Zhang Jue, Xie's merit was great and he ought to have been enfeoffed; Zhong slandered him; [9] Emperor Ling still remembered Xie's words, [10] did not add punishment, but in the end also did not enfeoff him; he was made Commandant of the Equipage in Anding.
translations.idiomatic: After Zhang Jue fell, Fu Xie deserved a fief; Zhao Zhong defamed him. Emperor Ling remembered Fu Xie’s earlier memorial [9][10], spared him punishment, denied him a noble rank, and named him commandant of the Equipage in Anding.

sentences.zh: 以疾免。

translations.literal: He was dismissed on account of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Illness forced him out of office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左傳曰，昔高陽氏有才子八人，蒼舒、隤摎、搗戭、大臨、尨降、庭堅、仲容、叔達，謂之八愷。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The Zuo Tradition says: Formerly the Gaoyang clan had eight talented men—Cang Shu, Tui Ao, Dao Yan, Da Lin, Pang Jiang, Ting Jian, Zhong Rong, Shu Da—called the eight harmonious.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zuozhuan* lists the “eight harmonious” worthies of Gaoyang.

sentences.zh: 高辛氏有才子八人，伯奮、仲堪、叔獻、季仲、伯虎、仲熊、叔豹、季狸，謂之八元也。

translations.literal: The Gaoxin clan had eight talented men—Bo Fen, Zhong Kan, Shu Xian, Ji Zhong, Bo Hu, Zhong Xiong, Shu Bao, Ji Li—called the eight primordial.
translations.idiomatic: The Gaoxin clan had eight “primordial” worthies—another classical roll call.

sentences.zh: 注[二]皇甫嵩傳曰：「連結郡國，自青、徐、幽、冀、荊、楊、兗、豫八州之人，莫不畢應。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Huangfu Song's biography says: "Linking commanderies and kingdoms—people from Qing, Xu, You, Ji, Jing, Yang, Yan, Yu across eight provinces—none failed to respond.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Huangfu Song’s biography says the uprising linked eight provinces.

sentences.zh: 」此云「六州」，蓋初起時也。

translations.literal: " This says "six provinces"—that refers to the beginning.
translations.idiomatic: The text’s “six provinces” reflects the earliest phase of the rebellion.

sentences.zh: 注[三]甫，始也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Fu means beginning.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Fu* means “only then” or “beginning.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]韓子曰「冰炭不同器而久，寒暑不同時而至」也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Han Feizi says "Ice and charcoal do not long share one vessel; cold and heat do not arrive in one season."
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Han Feizi* contrasts ice and charcoal in one vessel.

sentences.zh: 注[五]甘茂對秦武王曰：「昔曾參之居費，魯人有與曾參同姓名者殺人，人告其母曰『曾參殺人』，其母織自若也。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Gan Mao said to King Wu of Qin: "Formerly when Zeng Shen lived in Fei, a man of Lu with the same name as Zeng Shen killed a man; people told his mother 'Zeng Shen has killed a man'—his mother wove on undisturbed.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gan Mao’s parable of Zeng Shen’s mother—rumor repeated erodes even trust.

sentences.zh: 又告之，其母自若也。

translations.literal: They told her again; his mother was still undisturbed.
translations.idiomatic: They told her again; she kept weaving.

sentences.zh: 又告之，其母投杼下機，踰牆而走。

translations.literal: They told her again; his mother cast her shuttle, left the loom, leapt the wall and fled.
translations.idiomatic: A third report sent her dropping her shuttle and fleeing over the wall.

sentences.zh: 夫以曾參之賢與其母之信也，三人疑之，其母懼焉。

translations.literal: With Zeng Shen's worth and his mother's trust, three men's doubts—and his mother feared.
translations.idiomatic: Even Zeng Shen’s virtue and a mother’s faith broke under three lies.

sentences.zh: 」見史記也。

translations.literal: " It is seen in the Records.
translations.idiomatic: The story appears in the *Shiji*.

sentences.zh: 注[六]解見馬援傳。

translations.literal: Comment [6] Explanation is given in Ma Yuan's biography.
translations.idiomatic: See Ma Yuan’s biography for the “market tiger” reference.

sentences.zh: 注[七]白起與應侯有隙，構之秦昭王，免起為士伍，遷之陰密。

translations.literal: Comment [7] Bai Qi and Marquis Ying had a rift; he framed him before King Zhaoxiang of Qin, dismissed Qi to common soldier status, transferred him to Yinmi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Bai Qi fell out with Fan Ju; the king reduced him to common rank and banished him toward Yinmi.

sentences.zh: 行出咸陽西門十里，至杜郵，使賜□自裁。

translations.literal: He traveled out—ten li west of Xianyang's west gate, reached Duyou, and was made to [take] suicide.
translations.idiomatic: Ten li west of Xianyang he reached Duyou and was ordered to kill himself. (Graph damaged.)

sentences.zh: 見史記。

translations.literal: See Records.
translations.idiomatic: See the *Shiji*.

sentences.zh: 案杜郵，今咸陽城是其地。

translations.literal: Note: Duyou—modern Xianyang city is that place.
translations.idiomatic: Duyou lay where modern Xianyang stands.

sentences.zh: 酈元注水經雲渭水北有杜郵亭也。

translations.literal: Li Daoyuan's commentary on the Water Classic says north of the Wei is Duyou pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Li Daoyuan places Duyou pavilion north of the Wei.

sentences.zh: 注[八]殛音紀力反。

translations.literal: Comment [8] Ji pronunciation ji-li fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading for 殛: *ji-li* fan.

sentences.zh: 殛亦誅也。

translations.literal: Ji also means execute.
translations.idiomatic: *Ji* means “to strike dead” or execute.

sentences.zh: 注[九]續漢書曰：「燮軍斬賊三帥卜巳、張伯、梁仲寧等，功高為封首。」

translations.literal: Comment [9] Continued Book of Han says: "Xie's army beheaded the three bandit commanders Bo Yi, Zhang Bo, Liang Zhongning, etc.; merit ranked highest for enfeoffment."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Xu Han shu* credits Fu Xie with killing Bo Yi, Zhang Bo, and Liang Zhongning.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]識，記也，音志。

translations.literal: Comment [10] Shi means remember; pronunciation zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Shi* (“to remember”) reads like *zhi*.

sentences.zh: 後拜議郎。

translations.literal: Later he was appointed Consultant.
translations.idiomatic: He was later appointed gentleman consultant.

sentences.zh: 會西羌反，邊章、韓遂作亂隴右，征發天下，役賦無已。

translations.literal: It happened that the Western Qiang rebelled; Bian Zhang and Han Sui raised turmoil in Longyou; conscription was issued across the realm, corvée and taxes without cease.
translations.idiomatic: When the western Qiang rebelled and Bian Zhang and Han Sui seized Longyou, levies and taxes ran without end.

sentences.zh: 司徒崔烈以為宜□涼州。

translations.literal: Minister of Education Cui Lie thought it fitting to [discard] Liangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Minister of education Cui Lie urged abandoning Liang Province.

sentences.zh: 詔會公卿百官，烈堅執先議。

translations.literal: An edict assembled dukes, ministers, and all officials; Lie firmly held to his prior proposal.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor convened the court; Cui Lie pressed his plan.

sentences.zh: 燮厲言曰：「斬司徒，天下乃安。」

translations.literal: Xie spoke sharply: "Behead the Minister of Education and the realm will be settled."
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xie shouted, “Behead the minister of education and the realm will be at peace.”

sentences.zh: 尚書郎楊贊奏燮廷辱大臣。

translations.literal: Palace Gentleman Yang Zan memorialized that Xie had insulted a great minister in open court.
translations.idiomatic: Palace gentleman Yang Zan impeached Fu Xie for humiliating a senior minister.

sentences.zh: 帝以問燮。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon questioned Xie.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked Fu Xie to explain.

sentences.zh: 燮對曰：「昔冒頓至逆也，樊噲為上將，願得十萬觿橫行匈奴中，憤激思奮，未失人臣之節，顧計當從與不耳，季布猶曰『噲可斬也』。

translations.literal: Xie replied: "Formerly Modu was supremely contrary; Fan Kuai was senior general, wishing to get a hundred thousand hosts to range through the Xiongnu—in righteous wrath thinking to rouse himself, he did not lose a subject's integrity; one only considers whether the plan should be followed or not; Ji Bu still said 'Kuai can be beheaded.'
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xie answered: “When Modu insulted the Han, Fan Kuai vowed to ride a hundred thousand men across the steppe. Ji Bu still said Fan Kuai deserved execution—the issue is whether bravado serves the state.”

sentences.zh: [一]今涼州天下要磨，國家藩衛。

translations.literal: [1] Now Liangzhou is the realm's critical pivot, the state's screen and bulwark.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Liang Province is the empire’s pivot and the northwest shield.”

sentences.zh: 高祖初興，使酈商別定隴右；

translations.literal: When the Exalted Progenitor first rose, he sent Li Shang separately to settle Longyou;
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu sent Li Shang to secure Longyou;

sentences.zh: [二]世宗拓境，列置四郡，議者以為斷匈奴右臂。

translations.literal: [2] Shizong extended the border, established the four commanderies; debaters thought it severed the Xiongnu's right arm.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Emperor Wu pushed the frontier west and planted the four commanderies—“cutting off the Xiongnu’s right arm,” strategists said.

sentences.zh: [三] 今牧御失和，使一州叛逆，海內為之騷動，陛下臥不安寢。

translations.literal: [3] Now shepherds have lost harmony of control, making one province rebel; within the seas are stirred on its account; Your Majesty cannot sleep peacefully.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Misrule has turned one province against you and shaken the empire—you lie awake at night.”

sentences.zh: 烈為宰相，不念為國思所以弭之之策，乃欲割□一方萬里之土，臣竊惑之。

translations.literal: As Minister of Education he does not ponder for the state policies to end it, but wishes to cut off [missing] ten thousand li of territory—your subject privately is baffled.
translations.idiomatic: Yet your chancellor would carve off the northwest rather than save it—I cannot fathom it.

sentences.zh: 若使左衽之虜得居此地，[四]士勁甲堅，因以為亂，此天下之至慮，社稷之深憂也。

translations.literal: If you let lapel-left barbarians occupy this territory, [4] with fierce troops and hard armor, and thereupon make trouble—this is the realm's greatest anxiety, the altars' deep worry.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Hand that ground to barbarians fully armed and you court disaster—the gravest danger to the dynasty.”

sentences.zh: 若烈不知之，是極蔽也；

translations.literal: If Lie does not know this, that is extreme blindness;
translations.idiomatic: If Cui Lie does not understand, he is blind;

sentences.zh: 知而故言，是不忠也。

translations.literal: if he knows and yet speaks—this is disloyalty.
translations.idiomatic: if he understands and still urges surrender, he is disloyal.

sentences.zh: 」帝從燮議。

translations.literal: " The emperor followed Xie's proposal.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted Fu Xie’s argument.

sentences.zh: 由是朝廷重其方格，[五]每公卿有缺，為觿議所歸。

translations.literal: Thereby the court valued his uprightness and measure, [5] whenever dukes and ministers had a vacancy, he was whom the hosts of opinion endorsed.
translations.idiomatic: The court admired his integrity [5]; whenever a high office fell vacant, officials looked to him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]冒頓，匈奴單于名也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Modu is a Xiongnu chanyu's name.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Modu was a chanyu’s name.

sentences.zh: 前書曰，季布為中郎將，單于為書嫚呂太后，呂太后怒，召諸將議之。

translations.literal: The previous book: Ji Bu was General of the Palace; the chanyu wrote insulting Empress Dowager Lü; she was angry and summoned generals to deliberate.
translations.idiomatic: The *Hanshu* tells how the chanyu insulted Empress Lü and her generals met.

sentences.zh: 將軍樊噲曰：「願得十萬觿，橫行匈奴中。

translations.literal: General Fan Kuai said: "I wish to get a hundred thousand hosts to range the Xiongnu."
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai demanded a hundred thousand men to strike the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 」諸將皆阿太后，以噲言為然。

translations.literal: " The generals all flattered the Empress Dowager—Kuai's words were deemed right.
translations.idiomatic: The generals flatters Lü and endorsed Fan Kuai.

sentences.zh: 布曰：「樊噲可斬也！
