translations.literal: Bu said: "Fan Kuai can be beheaded!
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu answered, “Execute Fan Kuai!”

sentences.zh: 夫以高帝兵三十萬困於平城，噲時亦在其中。

translations.literal: For Gaodi's three hundred thousand troops were trapped at Pingcheng, and Kuai was then among them.
translations.idiomatic: Gaodi’s three hundred thousand were trapped at Pingcheng—Fan Kuai was there.

sentences.zh: 今柰何以十萬觿橫行匈奴中！」

translations.literal: Now how can you with a hundred thousand hosts range the Xiongnu!"
translations.idiomatic: How dare he promise to ride roughshod over the steppe with a hundred thousand?”

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書，漢王賜酈商爵信成君，以將軍為隴西都尉，別定北地。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Previous book: Han King granted Li Shang title Xin Chengjun; as general he was Colonel of Longxi and separately settled Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gaozu made Li Shang lord Xin Chengjun and sent him to pacify Longxi and Beidi.

sentences.zh: 注[三]前書，武帝分武威、酒泉，置張掖、敦煌，謂之四郡。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Previous book: Emperor Wu split Wuwei and Jiuquan and established Zhangye and Dunhuang—called the four commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Emperor Wu carved Wuwei and Jiuquan and added Zhangye and Dunhuang—the “four commanderies.”

sentences.zh: 劉歆等議曰：「孝武帝北攘匈奴，降昆邪十萬之觿，置五屬國，起朔方，以奪其肥饒之地。

translations.literal: Liu Xin and others debated: "Emperor Xiaowu beat back the Xiongnu north, subdued Kunxie's hundred thousand hosts, set up five dependent states, raised Shuofang, to seize their fertile lands.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xin recalled how Emperor Wu drove the Xiongnu back, settled Kunxie’s people in five dependencies, and built Shuofang on their grazing lands.

sentences.zh: 東伐朝鮮，起玄菟、樂浪，以斷匈奴之左臂。

translations.literal: East he attacked Korea, raised Xuantu and Lelang, to sever the Xiongnu's left arm.
translations.idiomatic: Eastward he took Korea and founded Xuantu and Lelang—“breaking the Xiongnu’s left arm.”

sentences.zh: 西伐大宛，□*[三十]*六國，結烏孫，起敦煌、酒泉、張掖，以*

translations.literal: West he attacked Dayuan, subdued thirty-six states (number damaged in text), allied with Wusun, raised Dunhuang, Jiuquan, Zhangye, to—
translations.idiomatic: Westward he subdued the Tarim states (text corrupt), allied Wusun, and raised Dunhuang, Jiuquan, and Zhangye—

translations.literal: (gao)
translations.idiomatic: (text fragment “gao”)

sentences.zh: **[鬲]*婼羌，裂匈奴之右臂。

translations.literal: —split Ruoqiang, split the Xiongnu's right arm.
translations.idiomatic: —to split the Gao-Ruo corridor and sever the Xiongnu’s right arm.” (Text badly damaged in transmission.)

sentences.zh: 」婼音而遮反。

translations.literal: " Ruo pronunciation er-zhe fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading for 婼: *er-zhe* fan.

sentences.zh: 注[四]說文曰：「衽，衣衿也。」

translations.literal: Comment [4] Shuowen says: "Ren is the garment lapel."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shuowen* defines *ren* as the lapel—here “barbarians of the left lapel.”

sentences.zh: 注[五]方，正也。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Fang means upright.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Fang* means upright.

sentences.zh: 格猶標準也。

translations.literal: Ge also means standard.
translations.idiomatic: *Ge* also means a measuring line—here “integrity.”

sentences.zh: 頃之，趙忠為車騎將軍，詔忠論討黃巾之功，執金吾甄舉等謂忠曰：「傅南容前在東軍，有功不侯，故天下失望。

translations.literal: Shortly Zhao Zhong became General of Chariots and Cavalry; an edict ordered Zhong to assess merit in suppressing the Yellow Turbans; Bearer of the Golden Mallet Zhen Ju and others said to Zhong: "Fu Nanrong formerly in the eastern army had merit but no marquisate—therefore the realm was disappointed.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao Zhong became general of chariots and cavalry and was ordered to grade Yellow Turban rewards, Zhen Ju urged him: “Fu Xie earned no marquisate for the eastern campaign—the empire noticed.”

sentences.zh: 今將軍親當重任，宜進賢理屈，以副觿心。」

translations.literal: Now the general personally holds heavy responsibility—you ought to advance the worthy and straighten the crooked, to answer the hosts' hearts."
translations.idiomatic: “Use your power to reward the worthy and set justice right.”

sentences.zh: 忠納其言，遣弟城門校尉延致殷勤。

translations.literal: Zhong accepted their words and sent his younger brother Commandant of the Gates Zhao Yan to convey earnest kindness.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Zhong agreed and sent his brother Zhao Yan, commandant of the gates, with lavish gifts.

sentences.zh: 延謂燮曰：「南容少荅我常侍，萬戶侯不足得也。

translations.literal: Yan said to Xie: "Nanrong, if you would yield a little to our Regular Attendant, a ten-thousand-household marquis is not beyond getting."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Yan told Fu Xie, “Thank my brother the eunuch and a ten-thousand-household fief is yours.”

sentences.zh: 」燮正色拒之曰：「遇與不遇，命也；

translations.literal: " Xie with stern countenance refused him: "Meeting favor or not is fate;
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xie refused coldly: “Recognition is a matter of fate;

sentences.zh: 有功不論，時也。

translations.literal: Merit unrewarded is timing.
translations.idiomatic: Whether merit wins reward depends on the times.

sentences.zh: 傅燮豈求私賞哉！

translations.literal: How could Fu Xie seek private reward!
translations.idiomatic: Did Fu Xie ever chase a private payoff?

sentences.zh: 」忠愈懷恨，然憚其名，不敢害。

translations.literal: " Zhong bore still deeper resentment, yet feared his name and did not dare harm him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Zhong hated him all the more but dared not touch him—Fu Xie’s reputation protected him.

sentences.zh: 權貴亦多疾之，是以不得留，[一]出為漢陽太守。

translations.literal: Powerful and noble families also mostly hated him; therefore he could not remain—[1] he went out as Administrator of Hanyang.
translations.idiomatic: The great families blocked him from staying at court [1], so he was posted as governor of Hanyang.

sentences.zh: 注[一]一作「封」。

translations.literal: Comment [1] One text reads "enfeoff."
translations.idiomatic: Note: One manuscript reads “could not be enfeoffed” instead of “could not remain.”

sentences.zh: 初，郡將范津明知人，舉燮孝廉。

translations.literal: Earlier, commandery general Fan Jin was discerning of men and nominated Xie Filial and Incorrupt.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Fan Jin, who knew talent, had recommended Fu Xie as filial and incorrupt.

sentences.zh: 及津為漢陽，與燮交代，合符而去，鄉邦榮之。

translations.literal: When Jin became [governor of] Hanyang, he and Xie handed over duties—tallies matched and they parted; the village and state honored it.
translations.idiomatic: When Fan Jin became governor of Hanyang, he and Fu Xie exchanged seals in an orderly handover—folk praised the sight.

sentences.zh: 津字文淵，南陽人。

translations.literal: Jin's style is Wenyuan; he was a man of Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Jin, courtesy name Wenyuan, was from Nanyang.

sentences.zh: 燮善恤人，叛羌懷其恩化，並來降附，乃廣開屯田，列置四十餘營。

translations.literal: Xie was good at comforting people; the rebel Qiang cherished his kindness and transformation, and all came to surrender and attach; he then broadly opened military colonies and set up more than forty camps.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xie cared for the people; rebel Qiang who remembered his kindness surrendered. He opened military farms and built forty-odd camps.

sentences.zh: 時刺史耿鄙委任治中程球，球為通姦利，士人怨之。

translations.literal: At that time Inspector Geng Bi entrusted the Adjunct Cheng Qiu; Qiu practiced illicit profit-through-connection; gentlemen resented him.
translations.idiomatic: Inspector Geng Bi left affairs to adjutant Cheng Qiu, who took bribes—the gentry despised him.

sentences.zh: [一]中平四年，鄙率六郡兵討金城賊王國、韓遂等。

translations.literal: [1] In the fourth year of Zhongping, Bi led troops of six commanderies to campaign against Jincheng bandits Wang Guo, Han Sui, and others.
translations.idiomatic: [1] In Zhongping 4 (187), Geng Bi led six commanderies against Wang Guo and Han Sui in Jincheng.

sentences.zh: 燮知鄙失觿，必敗，諫曰：「使君統政日淺，人未知教。

translations.literal: Xie knew Bi had lost the hosts and would surely be defeated; he remonstrated: "The envoy has governed only a short time; the people have not yet known your instruction.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xie warned him: “You have ruled briefly—the people hardly know your rule.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『不教人戰，是謂□之。

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'Not to teach men war yet send them to fight is called [missing] them.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, “To send untrained men to war is to abandon them.”

sentences.zh: 』今率不習之人，越大隴之阻，將十舉十危，而賊聞大軍將至，必萬人一心。

translations.literal: ' Now you lead men unpracticed in war across the barrier of great Long—there will be ten attempts and ten perils; when the bandits hear the great army will arrive, they must unite ten thousand hearts as one.
translations.idiomatic: You march raw levies over Longshan—ten tries, ten dangers—while the rebels will unite against you.”

sentences.zh: 邊兵多勇，其鋒難當，而新合之觿，上下未和，萬一內變，雖悔無及。

translations.literal: Frontier troops are mostly brave; their edge is hard to withstand; but newly combined hosts, high and low not yet harmonized—should internal mutiny happen at the slightest chance, though you regret it there will be no reaching it.
translations.idiomatic: Frontier troops fight hard, but your army is new and divided—one mutiny and it will be too late.”

sentences.zh: 不若息軍養德，明賞必罰。

translations.literal: Better to rest the army and nurture virtue, make rewards clear and punishments sure.
translations.idiomatic: Better rest the men, build morale, and enforce clear rewards and punishments.

sentences.zh: 賊得寬挺，[二]必謂我怯，腢惡爭埶，其離可必。

translations.literal: The bandits gain leisure and ease [2]—they will surely say we are timid; wickedness and evil will contend for advantage; their splitting apart can be counted on.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Give the enemy breathing space and they will think us afraid; their factions will quarrel and split.”

sentences.zh: 然後率已教之人，討已離之賊，其功可坐而待也。

translations.literal: Afterward lead men already taught to punish bandits already divided—the merit can be awaited sitting.
translations.idiomatic: Then strike a divided foe with trained troops—victory will come easily.”

sentences.zh: 今不為萬全之福，而就必危之禍，竊為使君不取。

translations.literal: Now you do not seek the blessing of perfect safety but venture toward inevitable peril—I privately think the envoy should not take it.
translations.idiomatic: You trade certain safety for certain disaster—I urge you not to.”

sentences.zh: 」鄙不從。

translations.literal: " Bi did not follow.
translations.idiomatic: Geng Bi ignored him.

sentences.zh: 行至狄道，果有反者，先殺程球，次害鄙，賊遂進圍漢陽。

translations.literal: When they reached Didao, there were indeed mutineers; they first killed Cheng Qiu, next harmed Bi; the bandits thereupon advanced to besiege Hanyang.
translations.idiomatic: At Didao the army mutinied—Cheng Qiu died first, then Geng Bi—and the rebels marched on Hanyang.

sentences.zh: 城中兵少徹盡，燮猶固守。

translations.literal: Within the city troops were few and utterly exhausted; yet Xie still firmly defended.
translations.idiomatic: The city had almost no defenders left, yet Fu Xie held firm.

sentences.zh: 注[一]漢官曰，司隸功曹從事，即持中也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Han Offices says: Colonel Director of Retainers merit clerk adjutant—that is chizhong.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The adjutant corresponded to the rank *chizhong*.

sentences.zh: 注[二]挺，解也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Ting means slacken.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Ting* here means “slack” or “ease.”

sentences.zh: 時北*[地]*胡騎數千隨賊攻郡，皆夙懷燮恩，共於城外叩頭，求送燮歸鄉里。

translations.literal: At that time several thousand northern Beidi Hu horsemen followed the bandits attacking the commandery—all long cherished Xie's kindness; together outside the city they knocked their heads, begging to escort Xie home to his village.
translations.idiomatic: Thousands of Beidi Hu cavalry joined the siege—yet they owed Fu Xie kindness and begged to escort him safely home.

sentences.zh: 子干年十三，從在官舍。

translations.literal: His son Gan, age thirteen, followed in the official quarters.
translations.idiomatic: His thirteen-year-old son Fu Gan was with him in the yamen.

sentences.zh: 知燮性剛，有高義，恐不能屈志以免，進諫曰：「國家昏亂，遂令大人不容於朝。

translations.literal: Knowing Xie's nature was rigid and had high integrity, he feared he could not bend his will to escape—he advanced remonstrance: "The state is dark and chaotic; it has caused the great man not to be tolerated at court.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Gan knew his father’s iron sense of honor and urged him: “The court is rotten—you were driven out.

sentences.zh: 今天下已叛，而兵不足自守，鄉里羌胡[一]先被恩德，欲令□郡而歸，願必許之。

translations.literal: Now the realm has already rebelled, yet troops are insufficient to defend ourselves; the village Qiang and Hu [1] earlier received your kindness and virtue—they wish to make you [leave] the commandery and return; I beg you must permit it.
translations.idiomatic: The empire rises against us; we cannot hold this town. The Qiang remember your kindness—let them take you home.”

sentences.zh: 徐至鄉里，率厲義徒，見有道而輔之，以濟天下。

translations.literal: Slowly reach the village, rally righteous followers, see one who has the Way and assist him, to aid the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Return home, gather loyal men, and aid whatever legitimate cause emerges.”

sentences.zh: 」言未終，燮慨然而歎，呼干小字曰：「別成，[二]汝知吾必死邪？

translations.literal: " Before he finished speaking, Xie sighed with emotion and called Gan's childhood name: "Biecheng [2]—do you know I must die?
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xie cut him off with a sigh: “Biecheng—do you think I can survive?

sentences.zh: 蓋『聖達節，次守節』。

translations.literal: Generally "the sage comprehends integrity; next preserves integrity."
translations.idiomatic: The sage bends before necessity; lesser men cling to principle.”

sentences.zh: [三]且殷紂之暴，伯夷不食周粟而死，仲尼稱其賢。

translations.literal: [3] Moreover Tyrant Zhou of Yin was cruel—Boyi would not eat Zhou grain and died; Zhongni praised his worth.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Tyrant Zhou was vile—Boyi starved rather than serve Zhou—Confucius called him worthy.”

sentences.zh: [四]今朝廷不甚殷紂，吾德亦豈絕伯夷？

translations.literal: [4] Now the court is not extremely Tyrant Zhou; my virtue—how could it match Boyi?
translations.idiomatic: [4] This court is no Zhou tyranny—am I Boyi’s equal?

sentences.zh: 世亂不能養浩然之志，[五]食祿又欲避其難乎？

translations.literal: Age in chaos cannot nurture vast bright aspiration; [5] receiving salary yet wishing to avoid its hardships?
translations.idiomatic: [5] In chaos one cannot keep every lofty ideal—yet I took the state’s pay.

sentences.zh: [六]吾行何之，必死於此。

translations.literal: [6] Where shall I go—I must die here.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Where would I run? I die here.”

sentences.zh: 汝有才智，勉之勉之。

translations.literal: You have talent and wisdom—strive, strive.
translations.idiomatic: You have gifts—use them well.

sentences.zh: 主簿楊會，吾之程嬰也。

translations.literal: Chief Clerk Yang Hui is my Cheng Ying.
translations.idiomatic: Chief clerk Yang Hui will be your Cheng Ying—he will protect you.”

sentences.zh: 」[七]干哽咽不能復言，左右皆泣下。

translations.literal: " [7] Gan choked and could not speak again; those left and right all wept.
translations.idiomatic: ” [7] Fu Gan wept too hard to answer; every attendant cried.

sentences.zh: 王國使故酒泉太守黃衍說燮曰：「成敗之事，已可知矣。

translations.literal: Wang Guo sent former Jiuquan Administrator Huang Yan to persuade Xie: "Success and defeat—the matter is already knowable.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Guo sent former Jiuquan governor Huang Yan to offer terms: “You see how the wind blows.

sentences.zh: 先起，上有霸王之業，下成伊呂之勳。

translations.literal: If you rise first, above you complete hegemon-king enterprise; below you achieve the merit of Yi and Lü.
translations.idiomatic: Lead now and you may rival the ancient kings—or match Yi Yin and Lü Wang.

sentences.zh: 天下非復漢有，府君寧有意為吾屬師乎？

translations.literal: The realm is no longer Han's; would my lord consider becoming commander-in-chief for us?
translations.idiomatic: Han is finished—will you not command our armies?”

sentences.zh: 」[八]燮案□叱衍曰：「若剖符之臣，反為賊說邪！

translations.literal: " [8] Xie [slammed the case] and rebuked Yan: "You are a credential-bearing minister—yet speak for bandits!
translations.idiomatic: ” [8] Fu Xie struck his bench and shouted: “You hold imperial commission—yet plead for rebels!”

sentences.zh: 」遂麾左右進兵，臨陣戰歿。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he waved left and right to advance troops; facing battle he died in combat.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered the advance and fell fighting.

sentences.zh: 謚曰壯節侯。

translations.literal: Posthumous title Marquis Zhuangjie.
translations.idiomatic: He was posthumously titled marquis *Zhuangjie* (“martial integrity”).

sentences.zh: 注[一]燮，北地人，故雲鄉里也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Xie was a Beidi man—therefore says village.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Fu Xie was from Beidi—“home” means back there.

sentences.zh: 注[二]干集曰：「干字彥林。」

translations.literal: Comment [2] Gan's collected works says: "Gan's style is Yanlin."
translations.idiomatic: Note: Fu Gan’s collected works give his courtesy name as Yanlin.

sentences.zh: 注[三]左傳曰，曹公子臧曰：「前志有之，聖達節，次守節，下失節。」

translations.literal: Comment [3] Zuo Tradition: Prince Zang of Cao says: "Former records have it: the sage comprehends integrity; next preserves integrity; lowest loses integrity."
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zuozhuan* lists three grades of holding to principle.

sentences.zh: 注[四]史記曰，伯夷，孤竹君之子也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Records: Boyi was son of the lord of Guzhu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Boyi was a son of the ruler of Guzhu.

sentences.zh: 武王載文王木主伐紂。

translations.literal: King Wu carried King Wen's wooden spirit tablet to campaign against Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: King Wu marched against Shang with his father’s memorial tablet.

sentences.zh: 殷既平，伯夷恥之，義不食周粟，遂餓死。

translations.literal: When Yin was pacified, Boyi was ashamed of it; on principle he would not eat Zhou grain—thereupon he starved to death.
translations.idiomatic: After the conquest Boyi refused Zhou grain and starved.

sentences.zh: 論語曰，子貢問曰：「伯夷、叔齊何人也？

translations.literal: The Analects: Zigong asked: "What sort of men were Boyi and Shuqi?
translations.idiomatic: The *Analects* quotes Zigong asking about Boyi and Shuqi.

sentences.zh: 」孔子曰：「古之賢人也。」

translations.literal: " Confucius said: "Ancient worthies."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius called them ancient worthies.”

sentences.zh: 注[五]孟子曰：「養吾浩然之氣。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Mencius says: "Nurture my vast flood-like qi."
translations.idiomatic: Note: Mencius speaks of nurturing *haoran* qi.

sentences.zh: 」趙岐注曰：「浩然，天氣也。」

translations.literal: " Zhao Qi's commentary says: "Haoran is heaven's qi."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi glosses *haoran* as heaven-like breadth.”

sentences.zh: 注[六]左傳曰，子路曰「食焉不避其難」也。

translations.literal: Comment [6] Zuo Tradition: Zilu says "Receiving salary one does not flee its hardships."
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zilu says one who takes pay must face its dangers.

sentences.zh: 注[七]程嬰，解見馮衍傳也。

translations.literal: Comment [7] Cheng Ying—explanation see Feng Yan's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Cheng Ying’s story appears in Feng Yan’s biography.

sentences.zh: 注[八]師即君也。

translations.literal: Comment [8] Shi means lord.
translations.idiomatic: Note: “Commander” (*shi*) here means leader—lord.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰「作之君，作之師」也。

translations.literal: The Documents says "made them lords, made them teachers."
translations.idiomatic: The *Shang shu* says heaven appoints rulers and teachers.

sentences.zh: 干知名，位至扶風太守。

translations.literal: Gan was famous; his office reached Administrator of Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Gan became famous and rose to governor of Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 蓋勳字元固，敦煌廣至人也。

translations.literal: Gai Xun, styled Yuangu, was a man of Guangzhi in Dunhuang.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun, courtesy name Yuangu, came from Guangzhi in Dunhuang.

sentences.zh: [一]家世二千石。

translations.literal: [1] For generations his family held two-thousand-dan rank.
translations.idiomatic: [1] His family had held salaried office for generations.

sentences.zh: [二]初舉孝廉，為漢陽長史。

translations.literal: [2] First nominated Filial and Incorrupt; he became Chief Clerk of Hanyang.
translations.idiomatic: [2] First recommended as filial and incorrupt, he became chief clerk of Hanyang.

sentences.zh: 時武威太守倚恃權埶，恣行貪橫，從事武都蘇正和案致其罪。

translations.literal: At that time the Administrator of Wuwei relied on power and position, wantonly practiced greed and brutality; Adjunct Su Zhenghe of Wudu investigated and established his crimes.
translations.idiomatic: The Wuwei governor abused power; Su Zhenghe, adjutant from Wudu, impeached him.

sentences.zh: 涼州刺史梁鵠畏懼貴戚，欲殺正和以免其負，乃訪之於勳。

translations.literal: Inspector of Liangzhou Liang Hu feared the imperial kin and wished to kill Zhenghe to escape his burden—thereupon he consulted Xun.
translations.idiomatic: Inspector Liang Hu feared reprisal and wanted Su Zhenghe dead—he asked Gai Xun’s advice.

sentences.zh: 勳素與正和有仇，或勸勳可因此報隙。

translations.literal: Xun had always had enmity with Zhenghe; some urged Xun could thereby repay the rift.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun hated Su Zhenghe; friends urged him to settle scores.

sentences.zh: 勳曰：「不可。

translations.literal: Xun said: "It cannot be done.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun refused.

sentences.zh: 謀事殺良，非忠也；

translations.literal: Plotting affairs to kill a good man is not loyalty;
translations.idiomatic: Murdering a good man for advantage is not loyalty;

sentences.zh: 乘人之危，非仁也。

translations.literal: riding on others' peril is not benevolence."
translations.idiomatic: exploiting another’s danger is not humane.”

sentences.zh: 」乃諫鵠曰：「夫紲食鷹鳶欲其鷙，[三]鷙而亨之，將何用哉？

translations.literal: " Thereupon he remonstrated Hu: "To tie food eagles and kites wanting them fierce [3]—if fierce yet you boil them, what use is there?
translations.idiomatic: He told Liang Hu: “You fatten a hawk to hunt—then stew it when it bites?

sentences.zh: 」鵠從其言。

translations.literal: " Hu followed his words.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Hu spared Su Zhenghe.

sentences.zh: 正和喜於得免，而詣勳求謝。

translations.literal: Zhenghe rejoiced at escaping and went to Xun to thank him.
translations.idiomatic: Su Zhenghe came to thank Gai Xun.

sentences.zh: 勳不見，曰：「吾為梁使君謀，不為蘇正和也。

translations.literal: Xun would not see him, saying: "I plotted for Inspector Liang—not for Su Zhenghe.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun refused to receive him: “I acted for the inspector—not for you.”

sentences.zh: 」怨之如初。

translations.literal: " Resentment was as at first.
translations.idiomatic: His hatred of Su Zhenghe remained.

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]廣至，縣名，故城在今瓜州常樂縣東，今謂之縣泉堡是也。

translations.literal: [4] Comment [1] Guangzhi is a county name; the old city is east of modern Guazhou Changle County—now called Xianquan fort.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Note: Guangzhi lay east of present Changle in Guazhou—site called Xianquan fort.

sentences.zh: 注[二]續漢書曰：「曾祖父進，漢陽太守。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Continued Book of Han says: "Great-grandfather Jin was Administrator of Hanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Xu Han shu* names his great-grandfather Jin, governor of Hanyang.

sentences.zh: 祖父彪，大司農。

translations.literal: Grandfather Biao was Grand Minister of Agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Biao was grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.zh: 」謝承書曰：「父字思齊，官至安定屬國都尉。」

translations.literal: " Xie Cheng's book says: "Father's style is Siqi; office reached Commandant of the Dependent State in Anding."
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng names his father Siqi, who rose to commandant of Anding dependent state.

sentences.zh: 注[三]紲，系也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Xie means tether.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Xie* means “to tether.”

sentences.zh: 廣雅曰：「鷙，執也。

translations.literal: Guangya says: "Zhi means seize."
translations.idiomatic: The *Guangya* glosses *zhi* as fierce grasping.

sentences.zh: 」蒼頡解詁曰：「鳶，鴟也。

translations.literal: " Cangjie gloss says: "Yuan is kite."
translations.idiomatic: The *Cangjie* gloss calls the kite *yuan*.

sentences.zh: 」食音嗣。

translations.literal: " Si pronunciation for "feed."
translations.idiomatic: Reading for “feed”: *si*.

sentences.zh: 注[四]續漢書，中平元年，黃巾賊起，故武威太守酒泉黃雋被征，失期。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Continued Book of Han: first year of Zhongping, Yellow Turban bandits rose; former Wuwei Administrator Huang Juan of Jiuquan was summoned and missed the deadline.
translations.idiomatic: Note: In Zhongping 1 the Turban revolt led to summoning Huang Juan, former Wuwei governor, who arrived late.

sentences.zh: 梁鵠欲奏誅雋，勳為言得免。

translations.literal: Liang Hu wished to memorialize to execute Juan; Xun spoke for him and he was spared.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Hu meant to execute him; Gai Xun secured a reprieve.

sentences.zh: 雋以黃金二十斤謝勳，勳謂雋曰：「吾以子罪在八議，故為子言。

translations.literal: Juan used twenty jin of gold to thank Xun; Xun told Juan: "I thought your crime fell among the eight deliberations—therefore I spoke for you.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Juan offered twenty pounds of gold; Gai Xun said, “You qualified for the eight mitigations—that is why I spoke.”

sentences.zh: 吾豈賣評哉！

translations.literal: How could I sell judgment!
translations.idiomatic: I do not sell justice!”

sentences.zh: 」終辭不受。

translations.literal: " In the end he declined and did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: He refused the gold.

sentences.zh: 中平元年，北地羌胡與邊章等寇亂隴右，刺史左昌因軍興斷盜數千萬。

translations.literal: First year of Zhongping, Beidi Qiang and Hu with Bian Zhang and others raided and rebelled in Longyou; Inspector Zuo Chang embezzled several tens of millions under cover of military levy.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhongping 1 the Beidi Qiang joined Bian Zhang in Longyou; inspector Zuo Chang pocketed millions from war funds.

sentences.zh: [一]勳固諫，昌怒，乃使勳別屯阿陽以拒賊鋒，[二]欲因軍事罪之，而勳數有戰功。

translations.literal: [1] Xun firmly remonstrated; Chang was angry and thereupon made Xun separately encamp at Ayang to resist the bandits' edge [2]—wishing by military affair to convict him—yet Xun repeatedly had battle merit.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Gai Xun objected; Zuo Chang sent him to Ayang hoping to trap him [2]—yet Gai Xun kept winning battles.

sentences.zh: 邊章等遂攻金城，殺郡守陳懿，勳勸昌救之，不從。

translations.literal: Bian Zhang and others thereupon attacked Jincheng, killed Administrator Chen Yi; Xun urged Chang to save him—he did not follow.
translations.idiomatic: When Bian Zhang took Jincheng and killed Chen Yi, Gai Xun begged Zuo Chang to relieve the city—Chang refused.

sentences.zh: 邊章等進圍昌於冀，昌懼而召勳。

translations.literal: Bian Zhang and others advanced to besiege Chang at Ji; Chang feared and summoned Xun.
translations.idiomatic: Bian Zhang besieged Zuo Chang at Ji; Chang panicked and called Gai Xun.

sentences.zh: 勳初與從事辛曾、孔常俱屯阿陽，及昌檄到，曾等疑不肯赴。

translations.literal: Xun at first with Adjuncts Xin Zeng and Kong Chang were all encamped at Ayang; when Chang's summons arrived, Zeng and others doubted and were unwilling to go.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun camped at Ayang with Xin Zeng and Kong Chang; they hesitated to answer Zuo Chang’s call.

sentences.zh: 勳怒曰：

translations.literal: Xun angrily said:
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun raged:

sentences.zh: 「昔莊賈後期，穰苴奮□。

translations.literal: "Formerly Zhuang Jia was late to muster; Rangju raised the execution axe."
translations.idiomatic: “When Zhuang Jia came late, Sima Rangju drew the blade of justice.”

sentences.zh: [三]今之從事，豈重於古之監軍哉！

translations.literal: [3] Today's adjunct—is he weightier than the ancient army supervisor!
translations.idiomatic: [3] Are you adjutants above the old camp supervisors?”

sentences.zh: 」曾等懼而從之。

translations.literal: " Zeng and others feared and followed him.
translations.idiomatic: They followed him in fear.

sentences.zh: 勳即率兵救昌。

translations.literal: Xun immediately led troops to rescue Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun marched to relieve Zuo Chang.

sentences.zh: 到，乃誚讓章等，責以背叛之罪。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he then rebuked Zhang and others, blaming them with the crime of rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: He rebuked Bian Zhang’s men for rebellion.

sentences.zh: 皆曰：「左使君若早從君言，以兵臨我，庶可自改。

translations.literal: All said: "If Inspector Zuo had early followed lord's words and brought troops against us, perhaps we could have reformed ourselves.
translations.idiomatic: They answered: “Had Zuo Chang listened earlier, we might have surrendered.

sentences.zh: 今罪已重，不得降也。

translations.literal: Now our crimes are already heavy—we cannot surrender."
translations.idiomatic: Now our guilt is too deep—we cannot yield.”

sentences.zh: 」乃解圍而去。

translations.literal: " Thereupon they lifted the siege and departed.
translations.idiomatic: They broke off the siege and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 昌坐斷盜征，以扶風宋梟代之。

translations.literal: Chang sat guilty for embezzling military levies; Song Xiao of Fufeng replaced him.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Chang fell for embezzling war funds; Song Xiao of Fufeng succeeded him.

sentences.zh: [四]梟患多寇叛，謂勳曰：「涼州寡於學術，故屢致反暴。

translations.literal: [4] Xiao worried that bandits rebelled in great numbers; he said to Xun: "Liangzhou is poor in learning and scholarship—therefore it repeatedly brings rebellion and brutality.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Song Xiao feared endemic rebellion and told Gai Xun: “Liangzhou lacks education—that is why it keeps exploding.”

sentences.zh: 今欲多寫孝經，令家家習之，庶或使人知義。

translations.literal: Now I wish to write many copies of the Classic of Filial Piety, order every household to practice it—perhaps thereby causing people to know righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: He proposed flooding Liang with copies of the *Classic of Filial Piety* so every home might learn duty.

sentences.zh: 」勳諫曰：「昔太公封齊，崔杼殺君；

translations.literal: " Xun remonstrated: "Formerly Taigong was enfeoffed in Qi—Cui Zhu murdered his lord;
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun objected: “When Lu Shang took Qi, Cui Zhu still murdered his duke;

sentences.zh: 伯禽侯魯，慶父篡位。

translations.literal: Bo Qin was marquis of Lu—Qing Fu seized the throne.
translations.idiomatic: when Bo Qin ruled Lu, Qing Fu still usurped.

sentences.zh: [五]此二國豈乏學者？

translations.literal: [5] Could those two states have lacked scholars?
translations.idiomatic: [5] Were Qi and Lu short of scholars?”

sentences.zh: 今不急靜難之術，遽為非常之事，既足結怨一州，又當取笑朝廷，勳不知其可也。

translations.literal: Now you do not urgently pursue means to quiet calamity, rashly undertake extraordinary matters—you will enough knot resentment in one province, and moreover ought receive laughter from the court; Xun does not know this can be done.
translations.idiomatic: You skip real pacification for a stunt—you will alienate the province and make Luoyang laugh. I cannot approve.”

sentences.zh: 」梟不從，遂奏行之。

translations.literal: " Xiao did not follow; thereupon he memorialized and carried it out.
translations.idiomatic: Song Xiao ignored him and memorialized the plan anyway.

sentences.zh: 果被詔書詰責，坐以虛慢征。

translations.literal: Indeed he received an edict-letter of rebuke; he sat for empty negligence and was summoned.
translations.idiomatic: The court rebuked him and recalled him for incompetence.

sentences.zh: 時叛羌圍護羌校尉夏育於畜官，[六]勳與州郡合兵救育，至狐盤，為羌所破。

translations.literal: At that time rebel Qiang besieged Colonel Protecting the Qiang Xia Yu at the livestock office; [6] Xun combined with province and commandery troops to rescue Yu—reached Hupan, was defeated by Qiang.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Rebels trapped Colonel Xia Yu at the pasture depot; Gai Xun joined provincial troops but was routed at Hupan.

sentences.zh: 勳收余觿百餘人，為魚麗之陳。

translations.literal: Xun gathered remainder troops more than a hundred men, formed a fish-gleam formation.
translations.idiomatic: He rallied a hundred survivors into a “fish-scale” formation.

sentences.zh: [七]羌精騎夾攻之急，士卒多死。

translations.literal: [7] Qiang picked cavalry attacked urgently from both flanks; many soldiers died.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Elite Qiang cavalry closed from both sides and slaughtered his men.

sentences.zh: 勳被三創，堅不動，乃指木表[八]曰：「必屍我於此。

translations.literal: Xun received three wounds, firmly did not move; he pointed to a wood marker [8] saying: "You must leave my corpse here.
translations.idiomatic: Wounded thrice, he stood fast and pointed at a stake: “Leave my corpse here.”

sentences.zh: 」句就種羌滇吾[九]素為勳所厚，乃以兵扞觿曰：「蓋長史賢人，汝曹殺之者為負天。

translations.literal: Gouxu-branch Qiang Dianwu [9] had always been generously treated by Xun; he thereupon used troops to shield the hosts, saying: "Chief Clerk Gai is a worthy man—you who kill him will bear blame toward Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Dianwu of the Gouxu Qiang, whom Gai Xun had favored, tried to shield him: “Kill this good man and you offend heaven.”

sentences.zh: 」勳仰罵曰：「死反虜，汝何知？

translations.literal: " Xun looked up and cursed: "Dead rebel—what do you know?
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun shouted back: “Traitors—what do you know?

sentences.zh: 促來殺我！

translations.literal: Hurry and kill me!
translations.idiomatic: Kill me now!”

sentences.zh: 」觿相視而驚。

translations.literal: " The hosts looked at each other in alarm.
translations.idiomatic: The warriors stared, stunned.

sentences.zh: 滇吾下馬與勳，勳不肯上，遂為賊所執。

translations.literal: Dianwu dismounted and yielded horse to Xun; Xun refused to mount—thereupon was seized by the bandits.
translations.idiomatic: Dianwu offered his horse; Gai Xun refused and was captured.

sentences.zh: 羌戎服其義勇，不敢加害，送還漢陽。

translations.literal: The Qiang Rong admired his righteousness and courage, did not dare harm him, escorted him back to Hanyang.
translations.idiomatic: They respected his courage and returned him unharmed to Hanyang.

sentences.zh: 後刺史楊雍即表勳領漢陽太守。

translations.literal: Later Inspector Yang Yong immediately memorialized Xun to act as Administrator of Hanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Inspector Yang Yong named him acting governor of Hanyang.

sentences.zh: 時人饑，相漁食，勳調谷稟之，[一０]先出家糧以率觿，存活者千餘人。

translations.literal: At that time people starved, mutually fished and ate each other; Xun allocated grain to relieve them; [10] first issuing family grain to lead the hosts—those who survived were more than a thousand.
translations.idiomatic: During famine and cannibalism he issued grain—leading by donating his own stores—and saved over a thousand lives.

sentences.zh: 注[一]斷謂割截。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Duan means cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Duan* means “to cut off” embezzled funds.

sentences.zh: 注[二]阿陽，縣，屬天水郡。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Ayang is a county belonging to Tianshui commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Ayang County lay in Tianshui.

sentences.zh: 注[三]齊景公時，燕、晉侵齊，景公以司馬穰苴為將，扞之，仍令寵臣莊賈監軍。

translations.literal: Comment [3] In Duke Jing of Qi's time, Yan and Jin invaded Qi; Duke Jing made Sima Rangju general to resist them, and ordered favorite minister Zhuang Jia to supervise the army.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Zhuang Jia story—favorite arrives late to Rangju’s camp.

sentences.zh: 與穰苴期旦日會，賈素驕貴，夕時至，穰苴召軍正問曰：「軍法期而後者雲何？

translations.literal: He agreed with Rangju to meet at dawn; Jia was habitually arrogant and noble—evening hour he arrived; Rangju summoned the army judge and asked: "What does military law say about those late to rendezvous?
translations.idiomatic: Zhuang Jia appeared at dusk; Rangju asked the judge the penalty for lateness.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「當斬。

translations.literal: " He replied: "Execution.
translations.idiomatic: “Death.”

sentences.zh: 」遂斬賈以徇三軍。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he beheaded Jia and displayed to the three armies.
translations.idiomatic: Rangju executed Zhuang Jia before the host.

sentences.zh: 注[四]續漢書「梟」字作「泉」也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Continued Book of Han writes the character as "quan."
translations.idiomatic: Note: One manuscript reads Song Quan instead of Song Xiao.

sentences.zh: 注[五]崔杼，齊大夫。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Cui Zhu was a Qi grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Cui Zhu was a Qi noble.

sentences.zh: 齊莊公先通其妻，杼殺之。

translations.literal: Duke Zhuang of Qi first had affair with his wife; Zhu killed him.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Zhuang slept with Cui Zhu’s wife—Cui murdered him.

sentences.zh: 慶父，魯莊公弟。

translations.literal: Qing Fu was younger brother of Duke Zhuang of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Qing Fu was Duke Zhuang of Lu’s brother.

sentences.zh: 莊公子開立，是為愍公，慶父襲殺愍公。

translations.literal: Duke Zhuang's son Kai was established—this was Duke Min; Qing Fu attacked and killed Duke Min.
translations.idiomatic: When Duke Min succeeded, Qing Fu murdered him.

sentences.zh: 並見史記。

translations.literal: Both are seen in Records.
translations.idiomatic: See the *Shiji*.

sentences.zh: 注[六]前書尹翁歸傳曰：「有論罪輸掌畜官。

translations.literal: Comment [6] Previous book Yin Wenggui biography says: "Those sentenced for crimes were sent to herd at the livestock office.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Hanshu* mentions convicts herding at state pastures.

sentences.zh: 」音義曰：「右扶風畜牧所在，有苑師之屬，故曰畜官。

translations.literal: " Yin Yi says: "Right Fufeng's pasture grounds had stable masters—therefore called livestock office.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Fufeng’s royal pastures housed convict herdsmen.

sentences.zh: 畜音許救反。」

translations.literal: Chu pronunciation xu-jiu fan."
translations.idiomatic: Pronounce the word as *xu-jiu* (fan).

sentences.zh: 注[七]麗音離。

translations.literal: Comment [7] Li pronunciation li.
translations.idiomatic: Reading for 麗: *li*.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「王以諸侯伐鄭，鄭原繁、高渠彌奉公為魚麗之陳，先偏後伍，伍承彌縫。

translations.literal: Zuo Tradition says: "The king with feudal lords attacked Zheng; Zheng's Yuan Fan and Gao Qumi presented the duke in fish-gleam formation—first flank behind five, five inheriting seam.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zuozhuan* describes the “fish-scale” deployment at Zheng.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注曰：「此魚麗陳法也。」

translations.literal: " Du Yu's commentary says: "This is the fish-gleam formation method."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu identifies it as the fish-scale array.”

sentences.zh: 注[八]表，標也。

translations.literal: Comment [8] Biao means marker.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Biao* is a stake or marker.

sentences.zh: 注[九]句就，羌別種也。

translations.literal: Comment [9] Gouxu is a separate Qiang branch.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gouxu was a Qiang subgroup.

sentences.zh: 句音古侯反。

translations.literal: Gou pronunciation gu-hou fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading for 句: *gu-hou* fan.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]調猶發也。

translations.literal: Comment [10] Diao means issue forth.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Diao* here means “to issue” grain.

sentences.zh: 後去官，征拜討虜校尉。

translations.literal: Later he left office; was summoned and appointed Colonel Who Punishes the Caitiffs.
translations.idiomatic: He later resigned, then was summoned as colonel who punishes barbarians.

sentences.zh: 靈帝召見，問：「天下何苦而反亂如此？

translations.literal: Emperor Ling summoned him for audience and asked: "Why does the realm suffer that it rebels and disorders thus?
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ling asked why the empire revolted.

sentences.zh: 」勳曰：「幸臣子弟擾之。

translations.literal: " Xun said: "Favored ministers' sons and younger brothers disturb it.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun answered: “The favorites’ kin provoked it.”

sentences.zh: 」時宦者上軍校尉蹇碩在坐，帝顧問碩，碩懼，不知所對，而以此恨勳。

translations.literal: " At that time Upper Army Colonel Jian Shuo of the eunuchs sat present; the emperor turned to ask Shuo; Shuo feared, did not know how to answer—and on this account bore resentment toward Xun.
translations.idiomatic: Jian Shuo the eunuch was present; the emperor turned to him—he froze—and hated Gai Xun for exposing him.

sentences.zh: 帝又謂勳曰：「吾已陳師於平樂觀，多出中藏財物以餌士，何如？

translations.literal: The emperor again said to Xun: "I have already displayed troops at Pingle Lodge, brought forth much from the inner treasury to bait the soldiers—how is it?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor boasted of reviewing troops at Pingle and emptying the treasury to reward men.

sentences.zh: 」[一]勳曰：

translations.literal: " [1] Xun said:
translations.idiomatic: ” [1] Gai Xun replied:

sentences.zh: 「臣聞『先王耀德不觀兵。

translations.literal: "Your subject has heard: former kings display virtue, do not display arms.
translations.idiomatic: “The ancients displayed virtue, not arms.”

sentences.zh: 』[二]今寇在遠而設近陳，不足昭果毅，秪黷武耳。」

translations.literal: ' [2] Now the bandits are far yet you set near formation—it is insufficient to display fruit and firmness; it only profanes arms."
translations.idiomatic: [2] “The enemy is far—you parade troops at the capital. That shows no resolve—only misuse of force.”

sentences.zh: [三]帝曰：「善。

translations.literal: [3] The emperor said: "Good.
translations.idiomatic: [3] “Well said.”

sentences.zh: 恨見君晚，腢臣初無是言也。

translations.literal: I regret meeting you late—none of my servants first spoke thus.
translations.idiomatic: I wish I had met you sooner—no one told me this.”

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]中藏謂內藏也。

translations.literal: " Comment [1] Zhongcang means inner treasury.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhong cang* is the imperial vault.

sentences.zh: 注[二]國語曰：「穆王將征犬戎，祭公謀父諫曰：『不可。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Discourses of the States says: "King Mu was about to campaign against Dog Rong; Duke Zhaogong remonstrated: 'It cannot be done.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Guoyu* quotes Duke Zhaogong against King Mu’s campaign.

sentences.zh: 先王耀德不觀兵。

translations.literal: Former kings display virtue, do not display arms.
translations.idiomatic: “Ancient kings displayed virtue, not arms.”

sentences.zh: 』」韋昭注曰：「耀，明也。

translations.literal: " Wei Zhao's commentary says: "Yao means brightness.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Zhao glosses *yao* as “bright.”

sentences.zh: 觀，示也。」

translations.literal: Guan means show."
translations.idiomatic: *Guan* means “to display.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]左傳曰「戎昭果毅以聽之之謂武，殺敵為果，致果曰毅」也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Zuo Tradition says "Rong displays fruit and firmness to heed it—this is called martial; killing the enemy is fruit; achieving fruit is firmness."
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zuozhuan* defines martial vigor as *guoyi*.

sentences.zh: 勳時與宗正劉虞、佐軍校尉袁紹同典禁兵。

translations.literal: At that time Xun together with Director of the Imperial Clan Liu Yu and Colonel Assistant Yuan Shao jointly commanded forbidden troops.
translations.idiomatic: He served with Liu Yu and Yuan Shao commanding palace guards.

sentences.zh: 勳謂虞、紹曰：「吾仍見上，上甚聰明，但擁蔽於左右耳。

translations.literal: Xun said to Yu and Shao: "I repeatedly see the emperor—the emperor is very intelligent, only blocked and screened by those at his left and right.
translations.idiomatic: He told them: “The emperor is clever—only blinded by those beside him.

sentences.zh: 若共並力誅嬖倖，然後征拔英俊，以興漢室，功遂身退，豈不快乎！

translations.literal: If we together exert strength to execute the favorites, afterward campaign and raise the splendid, to revive Han's house—merit achieved, body retired—could it not be joyful!
translations.idiomatic: If we purge the favorites and recruit talent, we could revive Han—then retire in triumph!”

sentences.zh: 」虞、紹亦素有謀，因相連結，未及發，而司隸校尉張溫舉勳為京兆尹。

translations.literal: " Yu and Shao also always had plans—thereby they linked together; before it could issue, Colonel Director of Retainers Zhang Wen nominated Xun as Metropolitan Governor.
translations.idiomatic: They agreed—but Zhang Wen nominated Gai Xun as governor of the capital before they acted.

sentences.zh: 帝方欲延接勳，而蹇碩等心憚之，並勸從溫奏，遂拜京兆尹。

translations.literal: The emperor was just wishing to extend reception to Xun, yet Jian Shuo and others inwardly feared him—all urged following Wen's memorial—thereupon he was appointed Metropolitan Governor.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ling wanted Gai Xun at court; Jian Shuo feared him and pushed him into the Jingzhao post.

sentences.zh: 時長安令楊黨，父為中常侍，恃埶貪放，勳案得其臧千餘萬。

translations.literal: At that time Metropolitan Magistrate Yang Dang—his father was a Regular Attendant—relied on power and acted greedy and unrestrained; Xun investigated and obtained his corruption of more than ten million.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrate Yang Dang, son of a eunuch, embezzled wildly; Gai Xun documented tens of millions.

sentences.zh: 貴戚鹹為之請，勳不聽，具以事聞，並連黨父，有詔窮案，威震京師。

translations.literal: Noble kin all pleaded for him; Xun did not listen—fully reported the affair—together implicating Dang's father—there was an edict for exhaustive investigation—awe shook the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Great families begged him off; he impeached father and son—the capital trembled.

sentences.zh: 時小黃門京兆高望為尚藥監，幸於皇太子，太子因蹇碩屬望子進為孝廉，勳不肯用。

translations.literal: At that time Junior Yellow Gate Gao Wang of Jingzhao was Supervisor of Imperial Medicine, favored by the crown prince; the crown prince through Jian Shuo entrusted Wang's son Jin to be nominated Filial and Incorrupt—Xun refused to employ him.
translations.idiomatic: The heir’s favorite Gao Wang asked Jian Shuo to nominate his son; Gai Xun refused.

sentences.zh: 或曰：「皇太子副主，望其所愛，碩帝之寵臣，而子違之，所謂三怨成府者也。

translations.literal: Someone said: "The crown prince is deputy lord—Wang is whom he favors; Shuo is the emperor's favored minister—yet you disobey them—this is what is called three resentments completing a treasury.
translations.idiomatic: Advisers warned: “You would anger heir, favorite, and eunuch—three sources of hate.”

sentences.zh: 」勳曰：「選賢所以報國也。

translations.literal: " Xun said: "Selecting the worthy is the means to repay the state.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun said, “Nomination serves the state.

sentences.zh: 非賢不舉，死亦何悔！

translations.literal: If not worthy I do not nominate—even death, what regret!
translations.idiomatic: I will not recommend the unworthy—death would not change my mind!”

sentences.zh: 」勳雖在外，每軍國密事，帝常手詔問之。

translations.literal: " Though Xun was outside, whenever there were military or state secret affairs, the emperor often personally wrote edicts to ask him.
translations.idiomatic: Though posted away from court, he received secret imperial letters on policy.

sentences.zh: [二]數加賞賜，甚見親信，在朝臣右。

translations.literal: [2] Repeatedly he received rewards and gifts—was greatly trusted and favored—stood right of court ministers.
translations.idiomatic: [2] The emperor showered honors on him—no minister ranked higher.

sentences.zh: 注[一]府，聚也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Fu means gather.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Fu* means “accumulate”—three hatreds pile up.

sentences.zh: 注[二]續漢書曰：「是時，漢陽叛人王國，觿十餘萬，攻陳倉，三輔震動。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Continued Book of Han says: "At that time Hanyang rebel Wang Guo's hosts were more than ten thousand; they attacked Chencang—the Three Adjuncts were shaken.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Xu Han shu* mentions Wang Guo’s siege of Chencang.

sentences.zh: 勳領郡兵五千人，自請滿萬人，因表用處士扶風*[士]*孫瑞為鷹鷂都尉，桂陽魏傑為破敵都尉，京兆杜楷為威虜都尉，弘農楊儒為鳥擊都尉，長陵第五鑈為清寇都尉。

translations.literal: Xun led commandery troops five thousand men—personally requested to fill ten thousand—thereby memorializing to employ recluses: Fufeng scholar Sun Rui as Colonel Hawk-Kite, Guiyang Wei Jie as Colonel Who Breaks the Enemy, Jingzhao Du Kai as Colonel Who Overawes Caitiffs, Hongnong Yang Ru as Colonel Bird-Strike, Changling Diwu Nao as Colonel Who Clears Bandits.
translations.idiomatic: He led five thousand men, asked for ten thousand, and named Sun Rui, Wei Jie, Du Kai, Yang Ru, and Diwu Nao as colonels.

sentences.zh: 凡五都尉，皆素有名，悉領屬勳。

translations.literal: In all five colonels—all had long had fame—all received orders subordinate to Xun.
translations.idiomatic: These five famous officers all served under him.

sentences.zh: 每有密事，靈帝手詔問之。」

translations.literal: Whenever there were secret affairs, Emperor Ling personally wrote edicts to ask him."
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ling consulted him by personal edict on every secret matter.”

sentences.zh: 及帝崩，董卓廢少帝，殺何太后，勳與書曰：「昔伊尹、霍光權以立功，猶可寒心，足下小丑，何以終此？

translations.literal: When the emperor died, Dong Zhuo deposed the young emperor, killed Empress Dowager He—Xun wrote him a letter: "Formerly Yi Yin and Huo Guang wielded power to establish merit—still one could chill at heart—you, petty fellow—how can you end thus?
translations.idiomatic: After Ling’s death Gai Xun wrote Dong Zhuo: “Even Yi Yin and Huo Guang took cold risk—you are a thief—how will this end?

sentences.zh: 賀者在門，吊者在廬，可不慎哉！

translations.literal: Congratulations stand at the gate; condolences stand at the hut—can you not be cautious!
translations.idiomatic: Celebration and mourning wait at your door—tread carefully!”

sentences.zh: 」[一]卓得書，意甚憚之。

translations.literal: " [1] Zhuo received the letter—his mind greatly feared it.
translations.idiomatic: ” [1] Dong Zhuo was shaken.

sentences.zh: 征為議郎。

translations.literal: He was summoned as Consultant.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuo summoned him to the capital as consultant.

sentences.zh: 時左將軍皇甫嵩精兵三萬屯扶風，勳密相要結，將以討卓。

translations.literal: At that time General of the Left Huangfu Song's crack troops thirty thousand encamped at Fufeng; Xun secretly bound alliance with him—was about to campaign against Zhuo.
translations.idiomatic: He plotted with Huangfu Song’s thirty thousand at Fufeng to strike Dong Zhuo.

sentences.zh: 會嵩亦被征，勳以觿弱不能獨立，遂並還京師。

translations.literal: It happened that Song was also summoned; Xun because the hosts were weak could not stand alone—thereupon both returned to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Huangfu Song was recalled too; Gai Xun lacked strength alone—both returned to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 自公卿以下，莫不卑下於卓，唯勳長揖爭禮，見者皆為失色。

translations.literal: From dukes and ministers downward, none did not abase themselves before Zhuo—only Xun with long bow-stance contended ritual—those who saw all lost color.
translations.idiomatic: Officials groveled to Dong Zhuo; Gai Xun alone stood straight and faced him as an equal—onlookers went pale.

sentences.zh: 卓問司徒王允曰：「欲得快司隸校尉，誰可作者？

translations.literal: Zhuo asked Minister of Education Wang Yun: "I wish to obtain a swift Colonel Director of Retainers—who can do it?
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo asked Wang Yun who should be colonel of the retainers.

sentences.zh: 」允曰：「唯有蓋京兆耳。

translations.literal: " Yun said: "Only Metropolitan Gai."
translations.idiomatic: “Only Gai Xun.”

sentences.zh: 」卓曰：「此人明智有餘，然不可假以雄職。」

translations.literal: " Zhuo said: "This man has wisdom to spare—yet cannot be lent a fierce office."
translations.idiomatic: “He is too clever for a powerful post.”

sentences.zh: 乃以為越騎校尉。

translations.literal: Thereupon he made him Colonel of Elite Cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: He named him colonel of elite cavalry instead.

sentences.zh: 卓又不欲令久典禁兵，復出為穎川太守。

translations.literal: Zhuo also did not wish to let him long command forbidden troops—again sent him out as Administrator of Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo would not let him hold the guards long—sent him toward Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 未及至郡，征還京師。

translations.literal: Before he reached the commandery, he was summoned back to the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He was recalled before taking office.

sentences.zh: 時河南尹朱儁為卓陳軍事。

translations.literal: At that time Intendant of Henan Zhu Jun stated military affairs for Zhuo.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jun of Henan briefed Dong Zhuo on strategy.

sentences.zh: 卓折鑈曰：「我百戰百勝，決之於心，卿勿妄說，且污我刀。

translations.literal: Zhuo interrupted Jun saying: "I am hundred battles hundred victories—I decide in my heart—you do not rashly speak—and defile my blade.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo cut him off: “I never lose—keep your counsel or dirty my sword.”

sentences.zh: 」勳曰：「昔武丁之明，猶求箴諫，[二]況如卿者，而欲杜人之口乎？」

translations.literal: " Xun said: "Formerly King Wuding's enlightenment still sought admonition—[2] how much more one like you—and wish to stop people's mouths?"
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun said: “Even King Wuding welcomed criticism—and you silence counsel?”

sentences.zh: 卓曰：「戲之耳。

translations.literal: Zhuo said: "Jesting only.
translations.idiomatic: “Only joking.”

sentences.zh: 」勳曰：「不聞怒言可以為戲？

translations.literal: " Xun said: "Never heard angry words can be jest?
translations.idiomatic: “Threats are no joke.”

sentences.zh: 」卓乃謝鑈。

translations.literal: " Zhuo thereupon apologized to Jun.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo apologized to Zhu Jun.

sentences.zh: 勳雖強直不屈，而內厭於卓，不得意，疽發背卒，時年五十一。

translations.literal: Though Xun was strong and upright and did not bend—within he was weary of Zhuo, did not achieve his intent—a sore arose on his back and he died—at age fifty-one.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun defied Dong Zhuo but sickened with frustration—a carbuncle killed him at fifty-one.

sentences.zh: 遺令勿受卓賻贈。

translations.literal: He left instructions not to accept Zhuo's funeral gifts.
translations.idiomatic: He forbade accepting Dong Zhuo’s funeral gifts.

sentences.zh: 卓欲外示寬容，表賜東園秘器賵襚，送之如禮。

translations.literal: Zhuo wished outwardly to display tolerance—memorialized to grant eastern park secret vessels and burial clothes—sent him off according to rite.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo feigned generosity—petitioned imperial grave goods and a state funeral.

sentences.zh: 葬於安陵。

translations.literal: He was buried at Anling.
translations.idiomatic: He was buried at Anling.

sentences.zh: 注[一]孫卿子曰「慶者在堂，吊者在閭，福與禍鄰，莫知其門」也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Master Xun said: "Those who congratulate are in the hall; those who condole are in the lane; fortune neighbors calamity—no one knows which gate they enter."
translations.idiomatic: Note: Xun Qing warns that joy and grief arrive side by side.

sentences.zh: 注[二]武丁，殷王高宗也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Wuding is King Gaozong of Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: King Wuding is Shang king Gaozong.

sentences.zh: 謂傅說曰：「啟乃心，沃朕心。

translations.literal: He said to Fu Yue: "Open your heart; enrich my heart."
translations.idiomatic: The *Shang shu* text has Wuding say to Fu Yue: “Open your mind to enrich mine.”

sentences.zh: 」說復於王曰：「惟木從繩則正，後從諫則聖。

translations.literal: Yue replied to the king: "Only if wood follows the cord is it straight; only if the ruler follows remonstrance is he sage."
translations.idiomatic: Fu Yue answers: wood needs the ink-line; a king needs remonstrance.

sentences.zh: 」見尚書。

translations.literal: See the Documents.
translations.idiomatic: See the *Shang shu*.

sentences.zh: 子順，官至永陽太守。

translations.literal: His son Shun, office reached Administrator of Yongyang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Gai Shun rose to governor of Yongyang.

sentences.zh: 臧洪字子源，廣陵射陽人也。

translations.literal: Zang Hong, styled Ziyuan, was a man of Sheyang in Guangling.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong, courtesy name Ziyuan, came from Sheyang in Guangling commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]父旻，有幹事才。

translations.literal: [1] Father Min had capacity for practical service.
translations.idiomatic: [1] His father Zang Min was an able administrator.

sentences.zh: [二]熹平元年，會稽妖賊許昭起兵句章，[三]自稱「大將軍」，立其父生為越王，攻破城邑，觿以萬數。

translations.literal: [2] In the first year of Xiping, Kuaiji occult bandit Xu Zhao raised troops in Gouzhang, [3] styled himself "Grand General," set up his father Sheng as King of Yue, broke cities and towns, hosts counted in ten thousands.
translations.idiomatic: [2] In 172 CE the Kuaiji rebel Xu Zhao seized Gouzhang [3], proclaimed himself grand general, set his father on a “Yue” throne, and mustered tens of thousands.

sentences.zh: 拜旻揚州刺史。

translations.literal: Min was appointed Inspector of Yangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: The court made Zang Min inspector of Yangzhou.

sentences.zh: 旻率丹*

translations.literal: Min led Dan
translations.idiomatic: Zang Min led Danyang

translations.literal: yang)
translations.idiomatic: (scribal gloss completing the commandery name “Danyang”)

sentences.zh: **[陽]*太守陳夤擊昭，破之。

translations.literal: Administrator Chen Yin attacked Zhao and defeated him.
translations.idiomatic: Governor Chen Yin of Danyang struck Xu Zhao and broke his army.

sentences.zh: 昭遂復更屯結，大為人患。

translations.literal: Zhao thereupon again re-encamped and linked—greatly became a people's calamity.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Zhao rallied again and became a serious menace.

sentences.zh: 旻等進兵，連戰三年，破平之，獲昭父子，斬首數千級。

translations.literal: Min and others advanced troops, fought successively three years, pacified them, captured Zhao father and son, beheaded several thousand levels.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Min campaigned for three years, captured Xu Zhao and his son, and took thousands of heads.

sentences.zh: 遷旻為使匈奴中郎將。

translations.literal: Min was transferred to Colonel of the Xiongnu in the Court.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Min was promoted to colonel in the Xiongnu court.

sentences.zh: 注[一]射陽故城在今楚州安宜縣東也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Sheyang old city is east of modern Chuzhou Anyi County.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Old Sheyang lay east of present Anyi in Chuzhou.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰：「旻達於從政，為漢良吏，遷匈奴中郎將。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Xie Cheng's book says: "Min was expert at governing; he was a good official of Han; he was transferred to Colonel in the Xiongnu Court.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Xie Cheng praises Zang Min as a model official.

sentences.zh: 還京師，太尉袁逢問其西域諸國土地風俗人物種數，旻具荅言西域本三十六國，後分為五十五，稍散至百餘國。

translations.literal: Returned to the capital; Grand Commandant Yuan Feng asked him about the Western Regions' various states—land, customs, people, and seed-count; Min fully answered, saying the Western Regions originally were thirty-six states, later split to fifty-five, gradually scattered to more than a hundred states.
translations.idiomatic: Back in Luoyang, Grand Commandant Yuan Feng quizzed him on the Western Regions: Zang Min traced their growth from thirty-six to over a hundred polities.

sentences.zh: 大小，道裡近遠，人數多少，風俗燥濕，山川草木鳥獸異物名種不與中國同者，口陳其狀，手畫地形。

translations.literal: Large and small, road li near and far, people count many or few, customs dry or wet, mountains rivers grass trees birds beasts strange things name kinds not the same as China—he orally stated their forms, hand-drew the terrain.
translations.idiomatic: He described each realm’s size, distance, population, climate, and wildlife—sketching maps from memory.

sentences.zh: 逢奇其才，歎息言：『雖班固作西域傳，何以加此乎？

translations.literal: Feng marveled at his talent, sighed and said: 'Though Ban Gu made the Western Regions treatise, what could be added to this?'
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Feng exclaimed that Zang Min outdid Ban Gu’s *Xiyu zhuan*.

sentences.zh: 』」注[三]句章縣故城在今越州鄮縣西。

translations.literal: ' " Comment [3] Gouzhang County old city is west of modern Yuezhou Mao County.
translations.idiomatic: ” [3] Note: Gouzhang lay west of present Mao in Yuezhou.

sentences.zh: 十三州志云：「句踐之地，南至句無，其後並吳，因大城句，章伯功以示子孫，故曰句章。」

translations.literal: Thirteen Provinces Treatise says: "Gou Jian's land reached south to Gouwu; later annexing Wu, on that account greatly walled Gou for Zhang Bo's merit to show descendants—therefore called Gouzhang."
translations.idiomatic: The *Thirteen zhou zhi* explains the name Gouzhang from Gou Jian’s border wall.

sentences.zh: 洪年十五，以父功拜童子郎，[一]知名太學。

translations.literal: At age fifteen, by his father's merit he was appointed Child Gentleman, [1] known at the Imperial University.
translations.idiomatic: [1] At fifteen Zang Hong became a “child gentleman” thanks to his father and studied at the imperial academy.

sentences.zh: 洪體貌魁梧，有異姿。

translations.literal: Hong's physique was towering; he had unusual bearing.
translations.idiomatic: He was tall and striking.

sentences.zh: [二]舉孝廉，補即丘長。

translations.literal: [2] Nominated Filial and Incorrupt; he filled Jiqiu magistrate.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Recommended as filial and incorrupt, he became magistrate of Jiqiu.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]漢法，孝廉試經者拜為郎。

translations.literal: [3] Comment [1] Han law: Filial and Incorrupt who passed classics examination were appointed Gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Note: Han rules promoted classics scholars from filial-incorrupt nominees.

sentences.zh: 洪以年幼才俊，故拜童子郎也。

translations.literal: Hong because young age and outstanding talent—therefore appointed Child Gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Youth and talent earned him the rare child gentleman title.

sentences.zh: 續漢書曰「左雄奏征海內名儒為博士，使公卿子弟為諸生，有志操者加其俸祿。

translations.literal: Continued Book of Han says: "Zuo Xiong memorialized summoning famous scholars within the seas as academicians, ordered dukes' and ministers' sons to be students—those with aspiration and conduct received increased salary.
translations.idiomatic: The *Xu Han shu* tells how Zuo Xiong elevated boy scholars.

sentences.zh: 及汝南謝廉、河南趙建章年始十二，各能通經，雄並奏拜童子郎。

translations.literal: When Ru'nan Xie Lian and Henan Zhao Jianzhang at age twelve each could penetrate the classics—Xiong together memorialized them appointed Child Gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Twelve-year-olds Xie Lian and Zhao Jianzhang also earned child gentlemen posts.

sentences.zh: 於是負書來學，雲集京師」也。

translations.literal: Thereupon bearing books came to study—cloud-like gathered at the capital."
translations.idiomatic: Students flocked to Luoyang.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]魁梧，壯大之貌也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Kuiwu is appearance of stalwart greatness.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Kuiwu* describes imposing stature.

sentences.zh: 梧音吾。

translations.literal: Wu pronunciation wu.
translations.idiomatic: Reading for 梧: *wu*.

sentences.zh: 注[三]即丘，縣，屬琅邪國，故城在今沂州臨沂縣東南，即春秋之祝丘也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Jiqiu is a county belonging to Langya state; the old city is southeast of modern Yizhou Linyi County—i.e. Spring and Autumn's Zhuqiu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Jiqiu in Langya is southeast of present Linyi—old Zhuqiu.

sentences.zh: 中平末，□官還家，太守張超請為功曹。

translations.literal: At the end of Zhongping, [left] office and returned home; Administrator Zhang Chao invited him as merit clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Zhongping he left office; Zhang Chao, governor of his commandery, made him chief clerk. (Text damaged at the verb for leaving office.)

sentences.zh: 時董卓*

translations.literal: At that time Dong Zhuo
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo

translations.literal: kill)
translations.idiomatic: killed

sentences.zh: **[弒]*帝，圖危社稷。

translations.literal: ** the emperor, plotted endangering the altars.
translations.idiomatic: —murdered the emperor and menaced the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 洪說超曰：「明府歷世受恩，兄弟並據大郡。

translations.literal: Hong persuaded Chao: "My lord for generations has received favor; brothers together hold great commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong urged Zhang Chao: “Your family has long enjoyed imperial favor; you and your brother govern great commanderies.

sentences.zh: [一]今王室將危，賊臣虎視，此誠義士□命之秋也。

translations.literal: [1] Now the royal house is about to be in peril; treacherous ministers watch like tigers—this is truly the season for knights to [pledge] their lives.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The throne totters; usurpers watch like tigers—this is the moment for men of duty to give their lives.”

sentences.zh: 今郡境尚全，吏人殷富，若動桴鼓，可得二萬人。

translations.literal: Now the commandery territory is still whole, officials and people are rich and populous; if you beat the drum, you can get twenty thousand men.
translations.idiomatic: Our land is intact and populous—raise the alarm and you can field twenty thousand.

sentences.zh: 以此誅除國賊，為天下唱義，不亦宜乎！

translations.literal: With this to execute the state bandit, to be first in the realm in righteous uprising—is it not fitting!
translations.idiomatic: Strike the traitor and lead the loyalists—should you not act?”

sentences.zh: 」超然其言，與洪西至陳留，見兄邈計事。

translations.literal: " Chao thought his words excellent; with Hong he went west to Chenliu, saw elder brother Miao to plan matters.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chao agreed; he and Zang Hong went to Chenliu to plan with Zhang Miao.

sentences.zh: 邈先謂超曰：「聞弟為郡，委攻臧洪，洪者何如人？

translations.literal: Miao first said to Chao: "I heard you as commandery have entrusted campaign to Zang Hong—what sort of man is Hong?
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Miao asked, “You put your war plan in Zang Hong’s hands—what kind of man is he?”

sentences.zh: 」超曰：「臧洪海內奇士，才略智數不比於超矣。

translations.literal: " Chao said: "Zang Hong is a rare man within the seas; talent, strategy, and wisdom are not comparable to Chao."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chao said, “He is a rare talent—my better in every way.”

sentences.zh: 」邈即引洪與語，大異之。

translations.literal: " Miao thereupon led out Hong and conversed with him—greatly thought him unusual.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Miao met Zang Hong and was awed.

sentences.zh: 乃使詣兗州刺史劉岱、[二]豫州刺史孔□，[三]遂皆相善。

translations.literal: He thereupon sent him to visit Inspector of Yanzhou Liu Dai, [2] Inspector of Yuzhou Kong [missing], [3] and all got on well.
translations.idiomatic: He sent Zang Hong to Yanzhou’s Liu Dai and Yuzhou’s Kong Zhou (name damaged in text)—all became allies.

sentences.zh: 邈既先有謀約，會超至，定議，乃與諸牧守大會酸棗。

translations.literal: Miao had already first had a secret pact; it happened that Chao arrived, settled plan—thereupon with various shepherds and guardians greatly met at Suanzao.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Miao had already conspired; when Zhang Chao arrived they fixed the league and met at Suanzao.

sentences.zh: 設□場，將盟，既而更相辭讓，莫敢先登，鹹共推洪。

translations.literal: They set up a [missing] field—about to covenant—then successively declined and yielded—none dared first ascend—all together pushed Hong.
translations.idiomatic: They prepared the oath-ground; everyone deferred until they thrust Zang Hong forward.

sentences.zh: 洪乃攝衣升□，操血而盟曰：「漢室不幸，皇綱失統，賊臣董卓，乘釁縱害，禍加至尊，毒流百姓。

translations.literal: Hong then gathered his robes and ascended [the altar], grasped blood and covenanted, saying: "The Han house is unfortunate; the imperial net has lost its warp; treacherous minister Dong Zhuo, riding opportunity wantonly harms—calamity reaches the supreme one; poison flows to the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong mounted the altar, cut his palm, and swore: “Han’s cord is broken; Dong Zhuo has murdered the sovereign and poisoned the people.

sentences.zh: 大懼淪喪社稷，翦覆四海。

translations.literal: Greatly fearing sinking and losing the altars, shearing and overturning the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: We fear for the altars and the realm.”

sentences.zh: 兗州刺史岱、豫州刺史伷、陳留太守邈、東郡太守瑁、[四]廣陵太守超等，糾合義兵，並赴國難。

translations.literal: Inspector of Yanzhou Dai, Inspector of Yuzhou Zhou, Administrator of Chenliu Miao, Administrator of Dongjun Mao, [4] Administrator of Guangling Chao and others have gathered righteous troops together to hurry to the state's hardship.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Dai, Kong Zhou, Zhang Miao, Qiao Mao, Zhang Chao, and others raise loyal armies to save the dynasty.”

sentences.zh: [五]凡我同盟，齊心一力，以致臣節，隕首喪元，必無二志。

translations.literal: [5] All we who covenant together—unite heart and single strength—to manifest subject integrity; lose head and ruin body—must have no second intent.
translations.idiomatic: [5] We swear one purpose—to serve Han unto death.”

sentences.zh: 有渝此盟，俾墜其命，無克遺育。

translations.literal: Who violates this covenant—let him lose his life; let him not keep offspring.
translations.idiomatic: Whoever breaks this oath dies without heirs.”

sentences.zh: [六]皇天后土，祖宗明靈，實皆鑒之。

translations.literal: [6] August Heaven and sovereign Earth, ancestral spirits bright—truly all oversee it.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Heaven, earth, and ancestors witness us.”

sentences.zh: 」洪辭氣慷慨，聞其言者，無不激揚。

translations.literal: " Hong's words and tone were stirring—those who heard his words, none were not roused.
translations.idiomatic: His voice shook every listener.

sentences.zh: 自是之後，諸軍各懷□疑，莫適先進，遂使糧儲單竭，兵觿乖散。

translations.literal: From this afterward, the various armies each harbored [missing] doubt—none suited to advance first—thereupon caused grain stores singly exhausted, troops scattered at odds.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward each army hesitated; supplies ran out and the coalition scattered.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謂超為廣陵，兄邈為陳留也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Refers to Chao as Guangling, elder brother Miao as Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zhang Chao governed Guangling; Zhang Miao Chenliu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]岱字公山。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Dai's style is Gongshan.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Liu Dai’s courtesy name was Gongshan.

sentences.zh: 注[三]伷字公緒。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Zhou's style is Gongxu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Kong Zhou’s courtesy name was Gongxu.

sentences.zh: 注[四]橋瑁也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] That is Qiao Mao.
translations.idiomatic: Note: “Mao” is Qiao Mao.

sentences.zh: 注[五]糾，收也。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Jiu means gather.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Jiu* means “to gather.”

sentences.zh: 注[六]左傳曰，王子虎盟諸侯於王廷，要言曰「皆□王室，無相害也。

translations.literal: Comment [6] Zuo Tradition: Prince Hu of Zhou covenanted feudal lords at the royal court; binding words said: "All [aid] the royal house—do not harm each other.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zuozhuan* oath language about aiding the Zhou house.

sentences.zh: 有渝此盟，明神殛之，俾墜其師，無克祚國」也。

translations.literal: Who violates this covenant—bright spirits strike him—let him lose his hosts—let him not inherit state."
translations.idiomatic: Breakers forfeit armies and succession.”

sentences.zh: 時討虜校尉公孫瓚與大司馬劉虞有隙，超乃遣洪詣虞，共謀其難。

translations.literal: At that time Colonel Who Punishes Caitiffs Gongsun Zan and Grand Marshal Liu Yu had a rift—Chao thereupon sent Hong to Yu to jointly plan that hardship.
translations.idiomatic: When Gongsun Zan feuded with Liu Yu, Zhang Chao sent Zang Hong to Liu Yu.

sentences.zh: 行至河閒而值幽冀交兵，行塗阻絕，因寓於袁紹。

translations.literal: When he reached Hejian he encountered You and Ji exchanging troops—the road was blocked—thereupon lodged with Yuan Shao.
translations.idiomatic: War between You and Ji blocked his path; he stayed with Yuan Shao.

sentences.zh: 紹見洪，甚奇之，與結友好，以洪領青州刺史。

translations.literal: Shao saw Hong—greatly thought him unusual—befriended him in alliance—appointed Hong to act as Inspector of Qingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao admired him, befriended him, and named him acting inspector of Qingzhou.

sentences.zh: 前刺史焦和好立虛譽，能清談。

translations.literal: Former Inspector Jiao He loved to establish empty fame; could pure converse.
translations.idiomatic: The previous inspector Jiao He cared only for empty reputation and clever talk.

sentences.zh: 時黃巾腢盜處處蹣起，而青部殷實，軍革尚觿。

translations.literal: At that time Yellow Turbans' gang bandits everywhere rose in riot—yet Qing's region was rich and solid—military gear was still abundant.
translations.idiomatic: While Turbans ravaged the empire, Qingzhou remained rich and armed.

sentences.zh: 和欲與諸同盟西赴京師，未及得行，而賊已屠城邑。

translations.literal: He wished with various allies to go west to the capital—before he could act—yet bandits had already slaughtered cities.
translations.idiomatic: Jiao He meant to march west but lost towns before he moved.

sentences.zh: 和不理戎警，但坐列巫史，禜禱腢神。

translations.literal: He did not manage military alert—only sat arranging witches and scribes, offering sacrifice praying [corrupt] gods.
translations.idiomatic: He ignored defense—only lined up shamans and prayed.

sentences.zh: [一]又恐賊乘凍而過，命多作陷冰丸，以投於河。

translations.literal: [1] Also he feared bandits would cross on frozen [river]—ordered many made trap-ice pellets—to cast into the river.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He threw “ice-cracking” pellets into the river to stop raiders—magical nonsense.

sentences.zh: 觿遂潰散，和亦病卒。

translations.literal: The hosts thereupon scattered in defeat—He also died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: His force collapsed; Jiao He died.

sentences.zh: 洪收撫離叛，百姓復安。

translations.literal: Hong gathered and soothed the scattered rebels—the hundred surnames returned to peace.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong rallied stragglers and restored order.

sentences.zh: 注[一]巫，女巫也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Wu means witch woman.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Wu* is a female shaman.

sentences.zh: 史，祝史也。

translations.literal: Shi means invoker scribe.
translations.idiomatic: *Shi* is the prayer clerk.

sentences.zh: 禜謂營攢用幣，以*

translations.literal: Ying means camp offerings using silk—to
translations.idiomatic: *Ying* means staged offerings to

translations.literal: rang)
translations.idiomatic: exorcise

sentences.zh: **[禳]*風雨霜雪水旱厲疫於日月星辰山川也。

translations.literal: ** wind rain frost snow drought plague pestilence regarding sun moon stars mountains rivers.
translations.idiomatic: —ward off weather and plague at nature shrines.

sentences.zh: 禱謂告事求福也。

translations.literal: Dao means reporting affairs seeking blessing.
translations.idiomatic: *Dao* is petitionary prayer.

sentences.zh: 在事二年，袁紹憚其能，徙為東郡太守，都東武陽。

translations.literal: In office two years—Shao feared his ability—transferred him to Administrator of Dongjun, capital at Dongwuyang.
translations.idiomatic: After two years Yuan Shao, fearing his talent, moved him to Dongjun at Dongwuyang.

sentences.zh: 時曹操圍張超於雍丘，甚危急。

translations.literal: At that time Cao Cao besieged Zhang Chao at Yongqiu—very urgent and perilous.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Cao besieged Zhang Chao at Yongqiu—desperate straits.

sentences.zh: 超謂軍吏曰：「今日之事，唯有臧洪必來救我。

translations.literal: Chao said to army clerks: "Today's matter—only Zang Hong will surely come save me.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chao told his officers: “Only Zang Hong will try to save us.”

sentences.zh: 」或曰：「袁曹方穆，而洪為紹所用，恐不能敗好遠來，違福取禍。

translations.literal: " Someone said: "Yuan and Cao are just harmonious—and Hong is employed by Shao—perhaps he cannot break friendship to come from afar—avoid blessing and take calamity.
translations.idiomatic: They answered: “Yuan Shao and Cao Cao are allies—Zang Hong serves Shao—he may not come.”

sentences.zh: 」超曰：「子源天下義士，終非背本者也，或見制強力，不相及耳。

translations.literal: " Chao said: "Ziyuan is the realm's knight of righteousness—in the end he is not one who betrays root—perhaps he is constrained by strong force—not able to reach."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Chao said: “Ziyuan is a man of honor—he would come if he could.”

sentences.zh: 」洪始聞超圍，及徒跣號泣，並勒所領，將赴其難。

translations.literal: " When Hong first heard Chao was besieged—he went barefoot wailing—together ordered those he led—would hurry to his hardship.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong heard the news barefoot and weeping—he tried to march.

sentences.zh: 自以觿弱，從紹請兵，而紹竟不聽之，超城遂陷，張氏族滅。

translations.literal: Since his hosts were weak—from Shao requested troops—yet Shao in the end did not heed him—Chao's city thereupon fell—Zhang clan was extinguished.
translations.idiomatic: Too weak to march alone, he begged Yuan Shao for troops—Shao refused—Yongqiu fell and the Zhangs perished.

sentences.zh: 洪由是怨紹，絕不與通。

translations.literal: Hong on this account resented Shao—utterly did not communicate with him.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong broke with Yuan Shao forever.

sentences.zh: 紹興兵圍之，歷年不下，使洪邑人陳琳以書譬洪，示其禍福，責以恩義。

translations.literal: Shao raised troops and besieged him—years passed without taking—sent Hong's townsman Chen Lin with letter to instruct Hong—showed him fortune and calamity—blamed him with kindness and righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao besieged him for years, then sent Chen Lin with a letter mixing threats and appeals.

sentences.zh: [一]洪荅曰： 注[一]獻帝春秋曰「紹使琳為書八條，責以恩義，告喻使降」也。

translations.literal: [1] Hong replied: Comment [1] Xiandi Chunqiu says: "Shao sent Lin to write eight articles—blamed him with kindness and righteousness—instructed him to surrender."
translations.idiomatic: [1] Zang Hong answered. Note: Yuan Shao had Chen Lin write eight arguments urging surrender.

sentences.zh: 隔闊相思，發於寤寐。

translations.literal: Separated wide—mutual longing—arises in waking and sleep.
translations.idiomatic: Though far apart I have dreamt of you.

sentences.zh: 相去步武，[一]而趨捨異規，其為愴恨，胡可勝言！

translations.literal: Separated only paces and strides [1]—yet tendency and choice differ in rule—its sorrowful regret—how can it be spoken exhaustively!
translations.idiomatic: [1] We stand yards apart in purpose—the ache is beyond words.

sentences.zh: 前日不遺，比辱雅況，[二]述□禍福，公私切至。

translations.literal: Former days you did not abandon—comparing insult elegant circumstances [2]—stating [missing] fortune and calamity—public and private cuttingly thorough.
translations.idiomatic: Your letter spelled out blessings and woes—earnest on every side.

sentences.zh: 以子之才，窮該典籍，豈將闇於大道，不達余趣哉？

translations.literal: With your talent—exhaustively mastering canons and chronicles—could you be dark about the great Way—not comprehend my intent?
translations.idiomatic: You know the classics—surely you grasp why I resist.

sentences.zh: 是以損□翰墨，一無所酬，亦冀遙忖褊心，粗識鄙性。

translations.literal: Therefore I diminished [missing] brush and ink—nothing to repay—I also hope distantly reckon narrow heart—roughly know base nature.
translations.idiomatic: I stayed silent hoping you would read my heart.

sentences.zh: 重獲來命，援引紛紜，雖欲無對，而義篤其言。

translations.literal: Again received coming command—citations numerous and tangled—although wishing not to answer—yet righteousness is thick in its words.
translations.idiomatic: Your new letter piles argument—I must answer for honor’s sake.

sentences.zh: 注[一]爾雅曰：「武，多也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Erya says: "Wu means many.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Erya* glosses *wu* as “many” for “paces.”

sentences.zh: 」 注[二]比，頻也。

translations.literal: " Comment [2] Bi means repeatedly.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Bi* means “repeatedly.”

sentences.zh: 僕小人也，本乏志用，中因行役，特蒙傾蓋，[一]恩深分厚，遂竊大州，寧樂今日自還接刃乎？

translations.literal: Your servant is a petty man—originally lacking aspiration and use—middle because traveling service—specially received canopy-tilt meeting [1]—kindness deep bond thick—thereupon stole great province—could I joyfully today turn myself to crossing blades?
translations.idiomatic: I am a minor man who owes you much—would I trade blows gladly?

sentences.zh: 每登城臨兵，觀主人之旗鼓，[二]瞻望帳幄，感故友之周旋，撫弦搦矢，[三]不覺涕流之覆面也。

translations.literal: Each time ascending walls facing troops—watching my lord's drums and banners [2]—gazing at tent curtains—moved by old friend's circling—stroking bow drawing arrow [3]—without noticing tears flowing covering face.
translations.idiomatic: Yet each time I see your camp I weep for old friendship—tears sheet my face.

sentences.zh: 何者？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 自以輔佐主人，無以為悔；

translations.literal: I thought that in assisting my patron I would have nothing to regret;
translations.idiomatic: I believed serving you left me nothing to regret;

sentences.zh: 主人相接，過絕等倫。

translations.literal: the patron's reception of me surpassed all comparison.
translations.idiomatic: your kindness exceeded anything I deserved.

sentences.zh: 受任之初，志同大事，埽清寇逆，共尊王室。

translations.literal: At the beginning of receiving commission, our aims agreed on great matters—sweep clear bandits and rebels—together honor the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: At first we shared one goal—purge rebels and restore Han.

sentences.zh: 豈悟本州被侵，郡將遘□，請師見拒，辭行被拘，使洪故君，遂至淪滅。

translations.literal: How could I realize my home province would be invaded, my commandery patron meet [missing] disaster—requesting troops I was refused—seeking leave I was detained—making Hong's former lord sink to ruin.
translations.idiomatic: Then my province burned; my patron Zhang Chao begged for aid—you refused and held me—until he perished. (Text damaged.)

sentences.zh: 區區微節，無所獲申，豈得復全交友之道，重虧忠孝之名乎？

translations.literal: Petty slight integrity—nowhere obtain declaration—how could I again preserve the way of friendship—doubly injure the name of loyalty and filial piety?
translations.idiomatic: My grievance goes unheard—how could I stay your friend and keep honor?

sentences.zh: 所以忍悲揮戈，收淚告絕。

translations.literal: Therefore I endure grief and brandish spear—hold tears and declare severance.
translations.idiomatic: So I take up arms and break with you in tears.
