sentences.zh: 若使主人少垂古人忠恕之情，來者側席，去者克己，[四]則僕抗季札之志，不為今日之戰矣。

translations.literal: If you had bent a little to the loyal forgiving heart of the ancients—those who came you seated aside mat—those who went you blamed yourself [four]—then your servant would resist Jizi's resolve—not wage today's battle.
translations.idiomatic: Had you shown ancient mercy—welcomed allies and blamed yourself—I might have gone like Jizi and spared this war.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]家語，孔子之郯，與程子相遇於塗，傾蓋而語也。

translations.literal: [5] Comment [1] Family Sayings: Confucius went to Tan—met Chengzi on the road—tilted canopy and conversed.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Note: The “tilted canopy” meeting comes from the *Kongzi jiayu*.

sentences.zh: 注[二]洪常寓於紹，故謂之主人也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Hong often lodged with Shao—therefore calls him master.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zang Hong served under Yuan Shao—“master” means Yuan Shao.

sentences.zh: 注[三]搦，捉也，音女卓反。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Nuo means grasp; pronunciation nu-zhuo fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Nuo* (“to grip”) reads *nu-zhuo* fan.

sentences.zh: 注[四]來者側席而待之，去者克己自責，不責人也。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Those who come—aside mat awaits them; those who go—blame themselves—not blame others.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The gloss urges generosity to guests and self-blame when they leave.

sentences.zh: 注[五]吳王余昧卒，欲授弟季札，季札逃去。

translations.literal: Comment [5] King Yuji of Wu died—wished to give younger brother Jizi—Jizi fled.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Jizi of Wu fled the throne—Zang Hong compares his choices.

sentences.zh: 見史記也。

translations.literal: Seen in Records.
translations.idiomatic: See the *Shiji*.

sentences.zh: 昔張景明登□喢血，奉辭奔走，卒使韓牧讓印，主人得地。

translations.literal: Formerly Zhang Jingming ascended [altar] sucked blood—received mandate and ran—finally making Inspector Han Fu yield seal—master gained the land.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Jingming swore the covenant and won Ji Province for Yuan Shao.

sentences.zh: 後但以拜章朝主，賜爵獲傳之故，不蒙觀過之貸，而受夷滅之禍。

translations.literal: Later only because of submitting memorial to court lord—granted noble rank receiving patent's reason—did not receive overlooking fault's pardon—yet received annihilation disaster.
translations.idiomatic: Later they died for a court title—no pardon—only annihilation.

sentences.zh: [一]呂奉先討卓來奔，請兵不獲，告去何罪，復見斫刺。

translations.literal: [1] Lu Fengxian campaigned against Zhuo and fled here—requested troops—not obtained—announced departure—what crime—again saw hacking stab.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Lu Bu fled Dong Zhuo, begged Yuan Shao for troops, was denied, tried to leave—and you tried to kill him.

sentences.zh: [二]劉子璜奉使踰時，辭不獲命，畏君懷親，以詐求歸，可謂有志忠孝，無損霸道，亦復殭屍麾下，不蒙虧除。

translations.literal: [2] Liu Zihuang carried mission exceeded time—resignation not obtained command—feared lord cherished kin—with deception sought return—can be called having aspiration loyalty filial—without harm to hegemon way—also again stiff corpse under banner—not granted fault removal.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Liu Zihuang overstayed his mission yet sought honorably to go home—you slaughtered him too.

sentences.zh: 慕進者蒙榮，違意者被戮，此乃主人之利，非游士之願也。

translations.literal: Those who admire advance receive glory—those who disobey intent are executed—this is the master's profit—not the wandering scholar's wish.
translations.idiomatic: Your followers thrive or die at your whim—that serves you, not them.

sentences.zh: 是以鑒戒前人，守死窮城，亦以君子之違，不適敵國故也。

translations.literal: Therefore taking mirror-warning from predecessors—guarding death in ruined city—is also because the gentleman's flight does not go to enemy state.
translations.idiomatic: So I die in this town—a gentleman does not flee to an enemy.
[3] End of that argument in Hong’s letter.

sentences.zh: 注[一]英雄記雲，袁紹使張景明、郭公則、高元才等說韓馥，使讓冀州與紹。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Yingxiong ji says: Yuan Shao sent Zhang Jingming, Guo Gongze, Gao Yuancai and others to persuade Han Fu—made him yield Ji Province to Shao.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yuan Shao used Zhang Jingming to squeeze Han Fu out of Ji.

sentences.zh: 然則馥之讓位，景明亦有其功。

translations.literal: Then Fu's yielding position—Jingming also had his merit.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Jingming earned part of the credit for Han Fu’s abdication.

sentences.zh: 其餘未詳也。

translations.literal: The rest is not detailed.
translations.idiomatic: Other details are unclear.

sentences.zh: 注[二]魏志呂布傳曰：「布破張燕軍而求益兵，觿將士鈔掠，紹患忌之。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Wei chronicle Lu Bu biography says: "Bu defeated Zhang Yan's army and sought increased troops—hosts and officers plundered—Shao feared and resented him.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Wei zhi* tells how Yuan Shao turned on Lu Bu.

sentences.zh: 布覺其意，從紹求去。

translations.literal: Bu perceived his intent—from Shao sought leave.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Bu saw the danger and asked to leave.

sentences.zh: 」英雄記：「布求還洛，紹假布領司隸校尉，外言當遣，內欲殺布。

translations.literal: " Yingxiong ji: "Bu sought return to Luoyang—Shao lent Bu to lead Colonel Director of Retainers—outwardly said would send—inwardly wished to kill Bu.
translations.idiomatic: The *Yingxiong ji* says Yuan Shao feigned a Luoyang posting to assassinate him.

sentences.zh: 明日當發，紹遣甲士三十人，辭以送布，止於帳側。

translations.literal: Next day when about to depart—Shao sent armored soldiers thirty men—pretext escorting Bu—stopped beside tent.
translations.idiomatic: Next day thirty guards “escorted” him—camp killers.

sentences.zh: 布偽使人於帳中鼓箏，紹兵臥，無何，出帳去而兵不覺。

translations.literal: Bu feigned having someone in tent play zither—Shao soldiers lay down—before long—left tent and soldiers did not notice.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Bu faked music, slipped out unseen.

sentences.zh: 夜半兵起，亂斫布默被，謂已死。

translations.literal: Midnight soldiers rose—chaotically hacked Bu's bedding—thought him already dead.
translations.idiomatic: At midnight they hacked his empty bed.

sentences.zh: 明旦，紹訊問，知布尚在，乃閉城門，布遂引去。」

translations.literal: Dawn—Shao interrogated—learned Bu still alive—thereupon closed city gates—Bu thereupon led away."
translations.idiomatic: At dawn Yuan Shao found him gone—Lu Bu escaped.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]左傳雲，公山不狃曰：「君子違不適讎國。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Zuo Tradition says: Gongshan Buniu said: "The gentleman in flight does not go to hostile state.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zuozhuan*—a noble exile avoids enemy soil.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「違，奔亡也。」

translations.literal: " Du Yu commentary says: "Wei means flee exile."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu glosses *wei* as “to flee.”

sentences.zh: 足下當見久圍不解，救兵未至，感婚姻之義，推平生之好，以為屈節而苟生，勝守義而傾覆也。

translations.literal: You ought to see long siege not lifted—rescue troops not arrived—moved by marriage obligation—pushed lifetime friendship—thought bending integrity to barely live—better than guarding righteousness and overturning.
translations.idiomatic: You think I should surrender for kinship and old ties rather than die upright.

sentences.zh: 昔晏嬰不降志於白刃，南史不曲筆以求存，[一]故身傳圖像，名垂後世。

translations.literal: Formerly Yan Ying did not lower will before white blades—Nan Shi did not bend brush to seek survival—[1] therefore body transmitted in images—name hangs posterity.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Yan Ying and the historians chose death over dishonor—names live on.

sentences.zh: 況僕據金城之固，驅士人之力，散三年之畜以為一年之資，匡困補乏，以悅天下，何圖築室反耕哉？

translations.literal: Moreover your servant relies on metal city's solidity—drives gentlemen's strength—scatters three years' stores to be one year's resources—rectifies want supplies lack—to please the realm—why plan build houses return plowing?
translations.idiomatic: I hold strong walls, three years’ grain, loyal men—why would I play farmer-soldier?

sentences.zh: [二]但懼秋風揚塵，伯珪馬首南向，[三]張揚、飛燕旅力作難，[四]北鄙將告倒懸之急，股肱奏乞歸之記耳。

translations.literal: [2] Only fear autumn wind raises dust—Bo Gui's horse head turns south—[3] Zhang Yang, Feiyan hosts exert force making trouble—[4] northern border will report inverted suspension's urgency—thigh and arm will memorialize begging return record.
translations.idiomatic: [2] But when Gongsun Zan rides south [3] and Zhang Yang and the Black Mountain bands rise [4]—your rear ignites—your generals will beg to go home.”

sentences.zh: [五]主人當鑒戒曹輩，反旌退師，何宜久辱盛怒，暴威於吾城之下哉！

translations.literal: [5] Master ought take mirror from Cao's sort—turn banners withdraw troops—what fitting long insult great wrath—violently display might beneath my walls!
translations.idiomatic: [5] Learn from Cao Cao’s retreat—do not waste fury on my walls!”

sentences.zh: 注[一]崔杼殺齊莊公，欲劫晏子與盟，以戟拘其頸，□承其心。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Cui Zhu killed Duke Zhuang of Qi—wished to force Yanzi to covenant—used halberd press his neck—[missing] confront his heart.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Cui Zhu threatened Yan Ying at swordpoint.

sentences.zh: 晏子曰：「劫吾以刃而失其意，非勇也。

translations.literal: Yanzi said: "Press me with blade and lose my intent—not courage.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Ying said coercion is not courage.

sentences.zh: 」崔杼遂釋之。

translations.literal: " Cui Zhu thereupon released him.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Zhu released him.

sentences.zh: 事見晏子。

translations.literal: Matters seen in Yanzi.
translations.idiomatic: See the *Yanzi chunqiu*.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「太史書曰『崔杼弒其君』，崔子殺之。

translations.literal: Zuo Tradition says: "Grand Scribe wrote saying 'Cui Zhu murdered his lord'—Cui master killed him.
translations.idiomatic: The *Zuozhuan* tells how scribes died writing “Cui Zhu assassinated his lord.”

sentences.zh: 其弟嗣書而死者二人，其弟又書，乃捨之。

translations.literal: His younger brother succeeded writing—two died—his younger brother again wrote—then spared.
translations.idiomatic: Brother after brother kept writing until Cui gave up.

sentences.zh: 南史氏聞太史盡死，執簡以往，聞既書矣，乃還」也。

translations.literal: Southern Scribe heard grand scribes all dead—carried bamboo slip and went—heard already written—then returned."
translations.idiomatic: A southern scribe arrived ready to die—the crime was already recorded.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]左傳曰：「楚子圍宋，築室反耕。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Zuo Tradition says: "Chu lord besieged Song—built houses returned plowing.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Chu besieged Song with long-term camps.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注曰：「築室於宋，反兵耕田，示無還意也。」

translations.literal: " Du Yu commentary says: "Built houses in Song—returned troops to plow fields—showed no return intent."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu: wall camps and farmed fields showed Chu would stay.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]伯珪，公孫瓚字。

translations.literal: Comment [3] Bo Gui is Gongsun Zan's style.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Bo Gui is Gongsun Zan’s courtesy name.

sentences.zh: 注[四]魏志曰，張揚字稚叔，雲中人也，以武勇給并州為從事。

translations.literal: Comment [4] Wei chronicle says: Zhang Yang styled Zhishu—a man of Yunzhong—by martial courage served Bingzhou as adjutant.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zhang Yang of Yunzhong served Bingzhou.

sentences.zh: 何進令於本州募兵，得千餘人，因留上黨擊山賊。

translations.literal: He Jin ordered him to recruit troops in home province—obtained more than a thousand men—thereupon stayed Shangdang attacking mountain bandits.
translations.idiomatic: He Jin sent him to recruit; he cleared Shangdang bandits.

sentences.zh: 進敗，揚遂以所將兵攻上黨，仍略諸縣，觿至數千，又與袁紹合。

translations.literal: Jin defeated—Yang thereupon with troops he led attacked Shangdang—still raided various counties—hosts reached several thousand—again allied with Yuan Shao.
translations.idiomatic: After He Jin fell, Zhang Yang raided Shangdang and joined Yuan Shao.

sentences.zh: 張燕，常山人，本姓褚。

translations.literal: Zhang Yan—Changshan man—originally surname Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Yan of Changshan was born surnamed Chu.

sentences.zh: 黃巾起，燕合聚少年為腢盜，觿萬人。

translations.literal: When Yellow Turbans rose—Yan gathered youths as gang bandits—hosts ten thousand.
translations.idiomatic: He raised ten thousand bandits when the Turbans rose.

sentences.zh: 博陵張牛角*

translations.literal: Boling Zhang Niujiao
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Niujiao of Boling

translations.literal: li)
translations.idiomatic: (scribal fragment)

sentences.zh: **[之]*起，觿次癭陶，牛角為飛矢所中，且死，告其觿曰：「必以燕為帥。

translations.literal: ** rose—hosts next Yingtao—Niujiao was struck by flying arrow—about to die—told his hosts: "Must make Yan commander.
translations.idiomatic: led a host to Yingtao; Zhang Niujiao fell to an arrow and named Zhang Yan successor.

sentences.zh: 」角死，觿奉燕，故改姓張。

translations.literal: " When Jiao died—hosts elevated Yan—therefore changed surname Zhang.
translations.idiomatic: They took Zhang as their surname.

sentences.zh: 燕僄悍，捷速過人，軍中號為「飛燕」。

translations.literal: Yan was nimble fierce—swift beyond others—in army titled "Flying Swallow."
translations.idiomatic: He was fast and fierce—“Flying Swallow.”

sentences.zh: 觿至百萬，號曰「黑山」。

translations.literal: Hosts reached a million—styled "Black Mountain."
translations.idiomatic: His force grew to a million—“Black Mountain.”

sentences.zh: 後助公孫瓚與紹爭冀州也。

translations.literal: Later aided Gongsun Zan contending Ji Province with Shao.
translations.idiomatic: Later they aided Gongsun Zan against Yuan Shao.

sentences.zh: 注[五]股肱猶手足也。

translations.literal: Comment [5] Thigh and arm are like hands and feet.
translations.idiomatic: Note: “Thigh and arm” means your trusted officers.

sentences.zh: 言北邊有倉卒之急，股肱之臣將告歸自救耳。

translations.literal: Says northern border has sudden urgency—thigh-arm ministers will memorialize begging return to save self.
translations.idiomatic: They will rush home if the north erupts.

sentences.zh: 足下譏吾恃黑山以為救，獨不念黃巾之合從邪？

translations.literal: You ridicule your servant relying on Black Mountain as rescue—alone do you not recall Yellow Turbans' vertical alliance?
translations.idiomatic: You mock my Black Mountain allies—did Gaozu scorn Peng Yue?

sentences.zh: 昔高袓取彭越於鉅野，[一]光武創基兆於綠林，卒能龍飛受命，中興帝業。

translations.literal: Formerly Gaozu took Peng Yue from Juye—[1] Guangwu founded omen-base at Lulin—finally able dragon fly receive mandate—middle revive imperial enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Gaozu used Peng Yue; Guangwu rose from Lulin—both took allies from rebels.

sentences.zh: 苟可輔主興化，夫何嫌哉！

translations.literal: If able assist lord raise transform—what dislike!
translations.idiomatic: If allies restore Han—why spurn them?

sentences.zh: 況僕親奉璽書，與之從事！

translations.literal: Moreover your servant personally received imperial seal-document—with them engaged in affairs!
translations.idiomatic: I hold imperial orders alongside them!

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書，彭越將其觿居鉅野中，無所屬，漢王乃使人賜越將軍印，使下濟陰以擊楚也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Previous book: Peng Yue led his hosts dwelling Juye—belonged to none—Han King thereupon sent person grant Yue general seal—ordered descend Jiyin to strike Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Han Gaozu commissioned Peng Yue from the marshes.

sentences.zh: 行矣孔璋！

translations.literal: Go, Kongzhang!
translations.idiomatic: Farewell, Chen Lin!

sentences.zh: 足下徼利於境外，臧洪投命於君親；

translations.literal: You seek profit beyond the border—Zang Hong casts life to lord and kin;
translations.idiomatic: You chase profit abroad—I give my life for kin and lord;

sentences.zh: 吾子托身於盟主，[一]臧洪策名於長安。

translations.literal: you sir entrust body to covenant lord—[1] Zang Hong inscribed name at Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: you serve Yuan Shao—I took office at Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 子謂余身死而名滅，僕亦笑子生死而無聞焉。

translations.literal: You say my person dies and name extinguishes—your servant also laughs at you—living and dying without fame.
translations.idiomatic: You predict my oblivion—I pity your empty fame.

sentences.zh: 本同末離，努力努力，夫復何言！

translations.literal: Root same branch separated—strive strive—what more to say!
translations.idiomatic: We began together—we end apart—so be it!

sentences.zh: 注[一]盟主謂袁紹也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Covenant lord means Yuan Shao.
translations.idiomatic: Note: “Covenant lord” is Yuan Shao.

sentences.zh: 紹見洪書，知無降意，增兵急攻。

translations.literal: Shao saw Hong's letter—knew no surrender intent—increased troops urgently attacked.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao saw Zang Hong would not yield and pressed the siege.

sentences.zh: 城中徹盡，外無援救，洪自度不免，呼吏士謂曰：「袁紹無道，所圖不軌，且不救洪郡將，洪於大義，不得不死。

translations.literal: Within city utterly exhausted—outside no rescue aid—Hong self reckoned could not escape—called clerks and gentlemen saying: "Yuan Shao lacks Way—what he plots is not upright—and did not save Hong's commandery patron—for Hong on great righteousness—cannot but die.
translations.idiomatic: When the city starved, Zang Hong told his men: Yuan Shao is faithless—he let Zhang Chao die—for honor I must die.

sentences.zh: 念諸君無事，空與此禍，[一]可先城未破，將妻子出。

translations.literal: Thinking of you gentlemen without affair—empty sharing this calamity—[1] can before city not yet broken—lead wives and children out.
translations.idiomatic: You are innocent—flee with your families before the walls fall.

sentences.zh: 」將吏皆垂泣曰：「明府之於袁氏，本無怨隙，今為郡將之故，自致危困，吏人何忍當捨明府去也？

translations.literal: " Generals and clerks all wept tears: "Bright lord toward Yuan clan—originally without resentment rift—now for commandery patron's reason—self brought peril difficulty—clerks and people how bear to abandon bright lord and go?
translations.idiomatic: They wept: “You owe Yuan Shao nothing—yet you die for Zhang Chao—we will not leave you.”

sentences.zh: 」初尚掘鼠，□筋角，後無所復食，主簿啟內廚米三斗，請稍為饘粥，[二]洪曰：「何能獨甘此邪？

translations.literal: " At first still dug rats—[missing] sinew horn—after nothing again to eat—chief clerk reported inner kitchen rice three dou—requested slightly make gruel porridge—[2] Hong said: "How can alone sweetly eat this?
translations.idiomatic: They ate rats and glue; three dou of rice remained—Zang Hong refused gruel alone.

sentences.zh: 」使為薄糜，□班士觿。

translations.literal: " Ordered make thin gruel—[missing] distribute to soldier hosts.
translations.idiomatic: He had thin gruel shared with every soldier.

sentences.zh: 又殺其愛妾，以食兵將。

translations.literal: Also killed his beloved concubine—to feed officers and generals.
translations.idiomatic: He killed his concubine to feed his troops.

sentences.zh: 兵將鹹流涕，無能仰視。

translations.literal: Officers and generals all wept tears—unable to look up.
translations.idiomatic: They wept too hard to look at him.

sentences.zh: 男女七八十人相枕而死，莫有離叛。

translations.literal: Men and women seventy or eighty people pillow-stacked died—none had leave rebel.
translations.idiomatic: Seventy or eighty starved together—none deserted.

sentences.zh: 注[一]與音預。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Yu pronunciation yu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The gloss gives the reading *yu* for the verb meaning “to share in” or “take part.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]杜預注左傳曰：「饘，糜也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Du Yu commentary Zuo Tradition says: "Zhan means thin gruel.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhan* is thin gruel.

sentences.zh: 」音之延反。

translations.literal: " Pronunciation zhi-yan fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading: *zhi-yan* fan.

sentences.zh: 城陷，生執洪。

translations.literal: City fell—alive seized Hong.
translations.idiomatic: The city fell; they took Zang Hong alive.

sentences.zh: 紹盛帷幔，大會諸將見洪。

translations.literal: Shao spread curtains—greatly assembled various generals to see Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao staged a full court before his generals.

sentences.zh: 謂曰：「臧洪何相負若是！

translations.literal: Said to him: "Zang Hong—how could you betray thus!
translations.idiomatic: He demanded: “How could you betray me?”

sentences.zh: 今日服未？」

translations.literal: Today submitted yet?"
translations.idiomatic: Will you yield now?”

sentences.zh: 洪據地瞋目曰：「諸袁事漢，四世五公，可謂受恩。

translations.literal: Hong leaned on ground glaring: "The Yuans served Han—four generations five dukes—can be called receiving favor.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong snarled: “The Yuans owe Han four generations of rank.

sentences.zh: 今王室衰弱，無扶翼之意，而欲因際會，觖望非冀，[一]多殺忠良，以立奸威。

translations.literal: Now royal house is weak and declining—without intention to support wings—yet wishes to ride opportunity—disappointed hope beyond hope—[1] much kills loyal good—to establish wicked might.
translations.idiomatic: The throne fails—yet you kill the loyal and build tyranny.

sentences.zh: 洪親見將軍呼張陳留為兄，則洪府君亦宜為弟，而不能同心戮力，為國除害，坐擁兵觿，觀人屠滅。

translations.literal: Hong personally saw general call Administrator Zhang of Chenliu elder brother—then Hong's prefect ought also be younger brother—yet could not unite heart join strength—for state remove harm—sit hugging troops—watch others slaughter annihilation.
translations.idiomatic: You called Zhang Miao “brother”—yet watched him die.

sentences.zh: 惜洪力劣，不能推刃為天下報仇，[二]何謂服乎？

translations.literal: Pity Hong strength weak—unable to push blade for realm revenge—[2] what called submit?
translations.idiomatic: I am too weak to avenge Han—do you call that submission?”

sentences.zh: 」紹本愛洪，意欲屈服赦之，見其辭切，知終不為用，乃命殺焉。

translations.literal: " Shao originally loved Hong—mind wished to bend him and pardon—saw his words cutting—knew in end would not be employed—thereupon ordered kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao loved him but saw he would never serve—had him executed.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前漢音義曰：「觖猶冀也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Former Han Yin Yi says: "Jue still means hope.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Jue* means “expect.”

sentences.zh: 」觖音羌恚反。

translations.literal: " Jue pronunciation qiang-hui fan.
translations.idiomatic: Reading for 觖: *qiang-hui* fan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]公羊傳曰：「事君猶事父也，父受誅，子復讎，推刃之道。」

translations.literal: Comment [2] Gongyang Tradition says: "Serving lord like serving father—father receives execution—son avenges—push blade Way."
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Gongyang* on vengeance for a father-lord.

sentences.zh: 洪邑人陳容，少為諸生，親慕於洪，隨為東郡丞。

translations.literal: Hong townsman Chen Rong—in youth was student—personally admired Hong—followed as Dongjun assistant.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Rong of Dongwu admired Zang Hong and served as his aide.

sentences.zh: 先城未敗，洪使歸紹。

translations.literal: Before city not defeated—Hong sent him return to Shao.
translations.idiomatic: Before the fall Zang Hong sent Chen Rong back to Yuan Shao.

sentences.zh: 時容在坐，見洪當死，起謂紹曰：「將軍舉大事，欲為天下除暴，而專先誅忠義，豈合天意？

translations.literal: At that time Rong sat present—saw Hong about to die—rose and said to Shao: "General raises great affair—wishes for realm remove violence—yet specially first executes loyalty and righteousness—how matches Heaven's intent?
translations.idiomatic: Chen Rong stood up: “You claim to save the empire yet murder the loyal first—does heaven approve?”

sentences.zh: 臧洪發舉為郡將，柰何殺之！

translations.literal: Zang Hong raised and recommended him to be commandery general—how can you kill him!
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong made you a general—how can you execute him?

sentences.zh: 」紹籩，使人牽出，謂曰：「汝非臧洪疇，空復爾為？

translations.literal: " Shao was ashamed—ordered men drag him out—said to him: "You are not Zang Hong's class—vainly again do this for what?
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao flushed; they dragged Chen Rong out. “You are not Zang Hong—why meddle?”

sentences.zh: 」容顧曰：「夫仁義豈有常所，蹈之則君子，背之則小人。

translations.literal: " Rong looked back: "Benevolence and righteousness—do they have a constant place? Tread them and you are a gentleman; turn your back and you are a petty man.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Rong said: “Right and wrong follow conduct—right makes a gentleman, wrong a coward.”

sentences.zh: 今日寧與臧洪同日死，不與將軍同日生也。

translations.literal: Today I would rather with Zang Hong die the same day—not with the general be born the same day.
translations.idiomatic: I would die with Zang Hong today rather than live with you.”

sentences.zh: 」遂復見殺。

translations.literal: " Thereupon again was killed.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao had him killed too.

sentences.zh: 在紹坐者，無不歎息，竊相謂曰：「如何一日戮二烈士！」

translations.literal: Those who sat in Shao's presence—none did not sigh—privately said to each other: "How in one day slaughter two heroic martyrs!"
translations.idiomatic: Everyone present whispered: “Two heroes killed in a single day.”

sentences.zh: 先是洪遣司馬二人出，求救於呂布。

translations.literal: Earlier Hong had dispatched two majors outward—to seek rescue from Lu Bu.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Zang Hong had sent two aides to beg Lu Bu for aid.

sentences.zh: 比還，城已陷，皆赴敵死。

translations.literal: By time they returned—the city had already fallen—all went toward enemy and died.
translations.idiomatic: They returned too late; both died fighting.

sentences.zh: 論曰：雍丘之圍，臧洪之感憤壯矣！

translations.literal: Treatise says: The Yongqiu siege—Zang Hong's stirring wrath was grand!
translations.idiomatic: The historian sighs: Yongqiu showed Zang Hong’s blazing grief.

sentences.zh: 想其行跣且號，束甲請舉，誠足憐也。

translations.literal: Imagine him going barefoot and wailing—binding armor requesting venture—truly enough pitiable.
translations.idiomatic: Barefoot, weeping, begging for troops—he earns pity.

sentences.zh: 夫豪雄之所趣捨，其與守義之心異乎？

translations.literal: As for what heroes abandon or keep—is it different from the heart that guards righteousness?
translations.idiomatic: Do heroes choose differently from honest men?

sentences.zh: 若乃締謀連衡，懷詐筭以相尚者，蓋惟利埶所在而已。

translations.literal: If then tying plots and linking alliances—harboring deceitful schemes to outdo each other—covertly only profit and power's place.
translations.idiomatic: Alliance politics chase power—not Hong’s pure motive.

sentences.zh: 況偏城既危，曹袁方穆，洪徒指外敵之衡，以紓倒縣之會。

translations.literal: Moreover isolated city already perilous—Cao and Yuan just harmonious—Hong vainly pointed to outer enemy's leverage—to relieve inverted-gallows crisis.
translations.idiomatic: His city was doomed; Cao and Yuan were friends—he could only bluff about other threats.

sentences.zh: 忿悁之師，兵家所忌。

translations.literal: Resentment-troops—what strategists avoid.
translations.idiomatic: Angry armies are what strategists shun.

sentences.zh: [一]可謂懷哭秦之節，存荊則未聞也。

translations.literal: [1] Can be said to embrace weeping-in-Qin integrity—preserving Jing not yet heard.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He had Bao Xu’s passion but not his success.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]前書魏相上書曰：「救亂誅暴，謂之義兵，兵義者王。

translations.literal: [2] Comment [1] Previous book Wei Xiang memorial said: "Saving chaos executing violence—is called righteous troops—troops righteous king.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Wei Xiang lists types of armies.

sentences.zh: 敵加於己，不得已而起者，謂之應兵，兵應者勝。

translations.literal: Enemy adds to self—unavoidably arise—these called responsive troops—responsive victorious.
translations.idiomatic: Defensive war wins.

sentences.zh: 爭恨小故，不勝憤怒者，謂之忿兵，兵忿者敗。

translations.literal: Struggling hatred small causes—not overcoming rage—these called wrath troops—wrath defeated.
translations.idiomatic: Angry armies lose.

sentences.zh: 利人土地貨寶者，謂之貪兵，兵貪者破。

translations.literal: Profiting from others' land goods treasures—these called greedy troops—greedy broken.
translations.idiomatic: Greedy armies crumble.

sentences.zh: 恃國家之大，矜其人觿，欲見威於敵者，謂之驕兵，兵驕者滅。

translations.literal: Relying on state's greatness—boasting its hosts—wishing to display might before enemy—these called arrogant troops—arrogant extinguished.
translations.idiomatic: Arrogant armies perish.

sentences.zh: 此非但人事，乃天道也。」

translations.literal: This not only human affairs—also Heaven's Way."
translations.idiomatic: These are heaven’s rules too.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]吳破楚，申包胥如秦乞師，立依於庭牆而哭，日夜不絕聲，勺飲不入口，七日秦師乃出，以車五百乘救楚，敗吳兵於稷。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Wu defeated Chu—Shen Baoxu went to Qin begging troops—stood leaning on courtyard wall and wept—day and night unbroken sound—spoon drink not entering mouth—seven days Qin troops then issued—five hundred chariots rescued Chu—defeated Wu troops at Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Shen Baoxu’s seven-day vigil moved Qin to save Chu.

sentences.zh: 事見左傳及史記。

translations.literal: Matters seen in Zuo Tradition and Records.
translations.idiomatic: See *Zuozhuan* and *Shiji*.

sentences.zh: 言臧洪徒守節致死，不能如包胥之存楚也。

translations.literal: Says Zang Hong vainly guarded integrity unto death—unable to match Baoxu's preserving Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong died for honor but could not save his cause like Bao Xu saved Chu.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：先零擾疆，鄧﹑崔□涼。

translations.literal: Encomium: Qianling disturbed the frontier—Deng and Cui [discarded] Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Encomium: Qianling shook the border; Deng Zhi and Cui Lie would abandon Liang.

sentences.zh: 詡﹑燮令圖，再全金方。

translations.literal: Xu and Xie laid plans—twice secured the golden frontier.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu and Fu Xie twice saved the northwest.

sentences.zh: 蓋勳抗董，終然允剛。

translations.literal: Gai Xun resisted Dong—finally thus upright and hard.
translations.idiomatic: Gai Xun defied Dong Zhuo to the death.

sentences.zh: 洪懷偏節，力屈志揚。

translations.literal: Hong cherished partial integrity—strength bent—will raised.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Hong’s stubborn loyalty failed his strength yet lifted his fame.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Textual collation notes

sentences.zh: 一八六六頁四行乃說李修曰按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁紀詡說太尉張禹，與傳異也。

translations.literal: Page 1866 line 4 "thereupon remonstrated Li Xiu" note: Collected explanations cite Hui Dong—the Yuan ji says Xu remonstrated Grand Commandant Zhang Yu—differs from biography.
translations.idiomatic: Editorial note: the *Yuan ji* names Zhang Yu instead of Li Xiu.

sentences.zh: 一八六七頁六行得朝歌何衰按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁紀「何衰」作「可哀」。

translations.literal: Page 1867 line 6 "getting Chaoge how unfortunate" note: Collected explanations cite Hui Dong—Yuan ji writes "how pitiable" instead of "how unfortunate."
translations.idiomatic: Variant reading: “pitiable” vs “unfortunate.”

sentences.zh: 一八六八頁二行譸當作籌也按：御覽一九０引正作「籌」，疑據章懷注改也。

translations.literal: Page 1868 line 2 "zhou should read chou" note: Imperial digest citation correctly writes "chou"—probably follows Zhang Huai’s commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Manuscript evidence supports reading *chou*.

sentences.zh: 一八六八頁一四行明日為三萬醋按：「三」原斗「二」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1868 line 14 "next day thirty thousand cu" note: "three" originally miswritten "two"—corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Numeral corrected from two to three.

sentences.zh: 一八六九頁五行築營壁百八十所汲本﹑殿本「百」上有「二」字。

translations.literal: Page 1869 line 5 "built camp ramparts one hundred eighty" Ji and Palace editions add "two" before "hundred."
translations.idiomatic: Some editions read two hundred eighty camps.

sentences.zh: 按：通鑒亦作「百八十所」。

translations.literal: Note: Comprehensive mirror also reads one hundred eighty.
translations.idiomatic: The *Zizhi tongjian* agrees with one hundred eighty.

sentences.zh: 一八六九頁九行自沮至下辯集解引惠棟說，謂案漢李翕碑題名，「辯」當作「辨」。

translations.literal: Page 1869 line 9 "from Ju to Xia Bian" collected explanations cite Hui Dong—Li Xi stele roster says "bian" should be "bian."
translations.idiomatic: Place-name variant 辯 vs 辨.

sentences.zh: 今按：續志作「辨」，通鑒胡注亦作「辨」。

translations.literal: Now note: Continued treatise writes "bian"—comprehensive mirror Hu commentary also "bian."
translations.idiomatic: Sources prefer 辨.

sentences.zh: 一八六九頁一五行每至春夏輒溢沒秋稼壞敗營郭按：類聚六引作「春夏輒潰溢，敗壞城郭」。

translations.literal: Page 1869 line 15 "each spring summer overflow drown autumn crops ruin camps outer walls" note: Classified anthology cites "spring summer burst overflow ruin wall cities."
translations.idiomatic: Parallel passage wording differs slightly.

sentences.zh: 一八七０頁一行石皆坼裂按：類聚引「坼」作「罅」。

translations.literal: Page 1870 line 1 "rocks all split" note: Classified anthology writes "crack" instead of "split."
translations.idiomatic: Lexical variant for splitting rock.

sentences.zh: 一八七０頁一行遂無泛溺之患按：汲本「泛」作「泛」。

translations.literal: Page 1870 line 1 "thereupon no flooding calamity" note: Ji edition "fan" written "fan."
translations.idiomatic: Homophone variants for flooding.

sentences.zh: 類聚引作「遂無沉溺之害」。

translations.literal: Classified anthology cites "thereupon no drowning harm."
translations.idiomatic: Quote variant.

sentences.zh: 一八七０頁二行谷石千集解引惠棟說，謂御覽八百六十五引續漢書，云「始到郡，谷千五百」，此脫「五百」字。

translations.literal: Page 1870 line 2 "grain one thousand" collected explanations cite imperial digest eight sixty-five citing Xu Han shu—says "beginning arrival grain one thousand five hundred"—here lacks "five hundred."
translations.idiomatic: Possible lacuna of “five hundred.”

sentences.zh: 今按：通鑒亦作「谷石千」。

translations.literal: Now note: Comprehensive mirror also reads "grain one thousand."
translations.idiomatic: The *Zizhi tongjian* keeps “one thousand.”

sentences.zh: 一八七０頁七行二府恐為臣所奏按：刊誤謂上文三公劾詡，則「二府」當為「三府」也。

translations.literal: Page 1870 line 7 "two offices fear minister will memorialize" correction says above three dukes impeached Xu—then "two offices" should be "three offices."
translations.idiomatic: Should read three offices, not two.

sentences.zh: 一八七０頁一四行敦字文理京*

translations.literal: Page 1870 line 14 "Dun style Wenli Jing
translations.idiomatic: Ms. fragment on Wang Dun’s name

translations.literal: zhao)
translations.idiomatic: (fragment)

sentences.zh: **[縣]*人也張森楷校勘記謂據順帝紀注，敦是河南京縣人，此「兆」字當衍文，或「縣」字之誤。

translations.literal: county man" Zhang Senkai collation says according to Shundi annals commentary Dun was Jingzhao Jing county man—this "zhao" character should be superfluous—or error for "county."
translations.idiomatic: Scholar argues "zhao" is stray text.

sentences.zh: 按：順帝紀注作「京縣人也」，今據改。

translations.literal: Note: Shundi commentary reads "Jing county man"—now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Emended to Jing county.

sentences.zh: 一八七一頁一行無令臣襲楊震之跡按：「楊」原斗「揚」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1871 line 1 "do not let minister follow Yang Zhen's footsteps" note: "Yang" originally miswritten "yang"—corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Graph corrected to Yang.

sentences.zh: 下一八七八頁六行「楊會」﹑一八八一頁二行「楊雍」﹑一八八三頁四行注「楊儒」同。

translations.literal: Below page 1878 line 6 "Yang Hui" page 1881 line 2 "Yang Yong" page 1883 line 4 commentary "Yang Ru" same.
translations.idiomatic: Cross-reference on Yang surname graphs.

sentences.zh: 一八七二頁七行自*[四]*百石以下陳景雲謂按續志「百石」上當有「四」字。

translations.literal: Page 1872 line 7 "from four hundred shi downward" Chen Jingyun says according to continued treatise "hundred shi" above should have "four."
translations.idiomatic: Missing “four” restored.

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now supplemented accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Added per commentary.

sentences.zh: 一八七二頁八行街*[裡]*走卒刊誤謂後漢志「街」下有一「裡」字。

translations.literal: Page 1872 line 8 "street ward runners" correction says Later Han treatise has extra "ward" under "street."
translations.idiomatic: Inserted missing 里.

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now supplemented accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Supplemented.

sentences.zh: 一八七二頁八行率皆赤幘縫□汲本﹑殿本「縫」作「絳」。

translations.literal: Page 1872 line 8 "generally all red kerchiefs sewn [missing]" Ji and Palace editions "sew" read "crimson."
translations.idiomatic: Kerchief color variant.

sentences.zh: 按：續志作「絳□」。

translations.literal: Note: Continued treatise reads "crimson [missing]."
translations.idiomatic: Parallel note.

sentences.zh: 一八七三頁一０行與左中郎*[將]*皇甫嵩刊誤謂案嵩傳，此少一「將」字。
